F3c-capability-drift.mechanism: MCP servers advertise tools via a JSON-RPC `tools/list` response and can push updates after initial approval using the `notifications/tools/list_changed` server-to-client notification. A malicious server serves a benign tool description at first connection to win the user's one-time approval, then later returns a mutated description (or silently changes server-side behavior bound to the same tool name) for the cached, already-trusted tool. Clients that cache approval against the tool name rather than a hash of the full manifest (name + description + input schema + behavior) inherit the prior consent, so the rewritten instruction reaches the model with no re-prompt.
F3c-capability-drift.exploitation_primitive: Operator stands up an MCP server that returns a clean manifest on the approval connection, then flips the tool description (or emits `tools/list_changed`) on a later session so the agent re-fetches a poisoned definition under inherited consent.

anchors.ref: Invariant Labs — MCP tool poisoning / rug-pull (MCP-Scan)
anchors.note: Original disclosure of post-approval tool-definition mutation and `list_changed`-driven rug-pull; tested 7 MCP clients, found Cursor vulnerable

anchors.ref: MCP spec — tools and tools/list_changed notification
anchors.note: Defines the `notifications/tools/list_changed` mechanism that lets servers update tool definitions after the client has loaded them

F3c-capability-drift.detection: Hash the full tool manifest (name + description + JSON input schema) at approval time and diff on every reconnect; alert on any `tools/list_changed` that mutates a previously-approved tool's description or schema. Pin approvals to the manifest hash, not the tool name.

references.title: Introducing MCP-Scan: Protecting MCP with Invariant
MCP Tools: Attack Vectors and Defense Recommendations — Elastic Security Labs

F4c-vector-eviction.mechanism: A RAG retriever ranks chunks by cosine similarity over embeddings and often blends recency/eviction heuristics into the index lifecycle. An attacker who can write to the corpus crafts documents whose embeddings sit maximally close to high-traffic query embeddings (adversarial decoding / hot-spot optimization) and re-writes them frequently, so the poisoned chunk both out-ranks benign competitors at retrieval and stays 'fresh' under recency-based eviction while genuine chunks age out. The result is poisoning-as-persistence: a small number of optimized records dominate the top-k for a class of queries indefinitely.
F4c-vector-eviction.exploitation_primitive: Operator submits embedding-optimized chunks (optionally re-touched on a timer to game recency) so retrieval consistently surfaces the poisoned record and evicts/down-ranks legitimate competitors for targeted queries.

anchors.ref: PoisonedRAG (USENIX Security 2025, Zou et al.)
anchors.note: Shows a handful of crafted texts injected into the knowledge DB achieve high attack success across similarity metrics and LLMs

anchors.ref: Adversarial Decoding (arXiv:2410.02163)
anchors.note: Generates readable documents optimized to maximize embedding similarity to target queries — the mechanism for out-ranking benign chunks

anchors.ref: MemoryGraft (arXiv:2512.16962)
anchors.note: Demonstrates persistent compromise where a small number of poisoned records dominate retrieved experiences on benign workloads

F4c-vector-eviction.detection: Monitor retrieval-result concentration: alert when a single document/source accounts for a disproportionate share of top-k hits across distinct queries, or when index churn shows one author re-writing near-duplicate high-similarity chunks. Provenance-tag chunks and gate retrieval eligibility on write-trust, not similarity alone.

references.title: PoisonedRAG — USENIX Security 2025
The Embedded Threat: Poisoning RAG Pipelines via Vector Embeddings — Prompt Security

F5c-memory-cross-tenant.mechanism: Agent long-term memory is typically backed by a shared vector index where retrieval eligibility is governed by embedding similarity, with tenant separation implemented as a soft metadata `namespace`/filter applied alongside (or after) the similarity search rather than as a hard partition. When filtering is applied post-retrieval, missing, or weakly keyed, a 'close enough' similarity collision returns chunks written by another tenant; hybrid search that blends a global index with a tenant index can likewise surface foreign memories. The crossing point is that read context is selected by vector distance, and the isolation predicate is not enforced as a precondition of the search.
F5c-memory-cross-tenant.exploitation_primitive: Operator (as tenant A) writes near-duplicate content tuned to match tenant B's expected queries, then triggers retrieval in a context where weak/post-hoc filtering lets B's secrets surface in A's read.

anchors.ref: Giskard — Cross-session leak in LLM applications
anchors.note: Documents cross-user data return in multi-tenant LLM systems lacking strong context isolation

anchors.ref: A Survey on the Security of Long-Term Memory in LLM Agents (arXiv:2604.16548)
anchors.note: Surveys isolation failures in long-term agent memory backends

F5c-memory-cross-tenant.detection: Enforce filter-before-retrieval (tenant predicate as a hard pre-filter on the ANN search, not a post-filter) and assert tenant-id equality on every returned chunk; alert when a retrieved record's owner tenant != requesting tenant. Per-tenant index/namespace separation at the infrastructure layer with no shared global fallback.

references.title: Cross Session Leak — Giskard
Shared Agent Memory: The Multi-Tenant Time Bomb

F6c-agent-token-forge.mechanism: Inter-agent protocols (e.g. Google A2A) carry tasking in JSON messages that may include a bearer/JWT credential identifying the calling agent. If the token is unsigned, has no verified `iss`/`aud`/`jti`, or is not sender-constrained, a rogue agent can mint or replay a credential and present forged peer identity to issue authoritative tasking. The trust boundary is crossed at JWT validation: a missing signature check or absent audience claim lets a token issued for (or stolen from) agent A be accepted by agent B as proof of authority.
F6c-agent-token-forge.exploitation_primitive: Operator captures or fabricates an A2A task credential lacking `aud`/`jti` binding and replays it against a peer agent to deliver attacker-chosen tasking under a trusted agent's identity.

anchors.ref: Google A2A Protocol security guidance
anchors.note: A2A delegates credential management to implementers; impersonation, card tampering and replay are real risks absent signing, audience binding, and jti replay caches

anchors.ref: AIP: Agent Identity Protocol for Verifiable Delegation (arXiv:2603.24775)
anchors.note: Proposes verifiable, audience-bound delegation across MCP and A2A to close exactly this forgery gap

F6c-agent-token-forge.detection: Require signed, short-lived, audience-bound tokens (verify `aud` == this agent, validate `iss`) and maintain a `jti` replay cache rejecting reused IDs until expiry; alert on tokens with absent/mismatched audience or repeated jti. Prefer sender-constrained tokens (mTLS-bound / DPoP).

references.title: Building a Secure Agentic AI Application Leveraging Google's A2A Protocol
A2A Protocol Security: Authenticating Agent-to-Agent Communication — SecureW2

F7d-detector-feedback-poison.mechanism: Continuously-tuned detection pipelines treat analyst dispositions (false-positive closures, suppression/allowlist additions, auto-tuning signals) as clean labels for retraining or threshold adjustment. An attacker generates activity that looks benign enough to earn repeated FP closures, training the model/rules to associate those exact features with 'normal'; the attacker then reuses the now-whitelisted pattern maliciously. This is a label-flipping / availability poisoning attack executed through the human-in-the-loop labeling channel rather than direct training-data access, shifting the model's decision boundary so the malicious pattern lands below threshold.
F7d-detector-feedback-poison.exploitation_primitive: Operator farms benign-looking telemetry tuned to a planned attack's feature signature until analysts close it as FP (or auto-suppression fires), then executes the real action reusing that conditioned pattern.

anchors.ref: Patching Deployed Malware Detectors / Passive-Aggressive FP patching (arXiv:2010.12080)
anchors.note: Shows analyst-FP-driven model patching can be steered, disturbing the FP/FN balance

anchors.ref: AI to protect AI: detecting label-flipping poisoning (ScienceDirect, 2025)
anchors.note: Characterizes label-flipping poisoning that maps to malicious analyst-disposition labels

F7d-detector-feedback-poison.detection: Audit disposition provenance: track which entities/sources accumulate FP-close labels and quarantine labels from low-trust or anomalously repetitive closers before retraining; monitor for decision-boundary drift toward a recently-frequent pattern. Keep a frozen holdout and replay closed-FP signatures against it before promoting tuning.

references.title: Getting Passive Aggressive About False Positives — arXiv:2010.12080
AI to protect AI: a modular pipeline for detecting label-flipping poisoning

F8c-skill-typosquat-install.mechanism: Agent skill/plugin registries resolve a requested skill by name/description with minimal publisher vetting and no cryptographic author binding, mirroring early npm/PyPI. An attacker registers a typosquatted skill name; the agent (or its operator) resolves the lookalike and installs it, at which point the skill's bundled scripts and natural-language instructions execute with the agent's full privileges — environment variables (API keys, tokens), filesystem, and network — often without explicit per-action approval. The boundary is crossed at name resolution: install-time trust is granted to the resolved artifact, not to a verified author identity.
F8c-skill-typosquat-install.exploitation_primitive: Operator publishes a typosquatted/slopsquatted skill to a low-vetting registry so an agent installing the intended-sounding name pulls and runs the attacker's bundle at agent+host authority.

anchors.ref: ClawHavoc / ClawHub malicious-skill campaign (Jan–Feb 2026)
anchors.note: Koi Security audit found 341 malicious entries among 2,857 skills on ClawHub traced to one coordinated campaign; Snyk ToxicSkills study found prompt injection in 36% of audited skills

anchors.ref: Towards Secure Agent Skills (arXiv:2604.02837)
anchors.note: Threat taxonomy for agent-skill supply chain including typosquat-at-install inheriting agent privileges

F8c-skill-typosquat-install.detection: Pin skills to a verified publisher identity / signature and a content hash; alert on install of names within small edit-distance of popular skills, and on first-run skills that read credential env vars or open egress. Scan bundled scripts for hallucinated/uncreated package names (slopsquat pre-claim) before install.

references.title: Snyk ToxicSkills study — malicious AI agent skills on ClawHub
Towards Secure Agent Skills: Architecture, Threat Taxonomy, and Security Analysis

X-identity-agent-audience.mechanism: In federated OIDC/OAuth, a downstream resource server trusts any token from a trusted issuer; if the token carries no `aud` (audience) or actor/`act` binding scoping it to a specific resource, it is accepted wherever issuer trust reaches. An agent that obtains a token minted for one context (or the human's own identity token) can present it to a backend it was never scoped for — a confused-deputy at the federation layer. RFC 8707 resource indicators fix issuance (the AS stamps `aud`), but the failure manifests when the resource server does not validate that `aud` matches its own URI, so a single token becomes valid across multiple resource servers.
X-identity-agent-audience.exploitation_primitive: Operator drives the agent to present an audience-unbound federated token (its own or the user's) to a higher-privilege backend that accepts it on issuer trust alone, crossing into resources outside the token's intended scope.

anchors.ref: RFC 8707 — Resource Indicators for OAuth 2.0
anchors.note: Defines the `resource` parameter and `aud` binding whose absence enables cross-resource token reuse (confused deputy)

anchors.ref: MCP Authorization spec — token passthrough prohibition
anchors.note: MCP servers must not pass client tokens upstream precisely because audience-unbound reuse is a confused-deputy vulnerability

X-identity-agent-audience.detection: At every resource server, reject tokens whose `aud` != the server's own resource URI; log and alert on tokens presented to a resource whose audience names a different service. Issue per-resource tokens via RFC 8707 resource indicators and short TTLs; flag any agent reusing one token across multiple distinct audiences.

references.title: RFC 8707: Resource Indicators for OAuth 2.0
Resource Indicators in OAuth 2.0 tighten MCP token boundaries

X-soc-rag-inject.mechanism: A SOC copilot retrieves from an internal corpus (tickets, alert comments, runbooks, case notes) assumed to be analyst-authored, embedding retrieved text directly into the LLM context with the same trust as system instructions. Low-trust users — or attackers who can file or comment on tickets — write indirect prompt-injection payloads into that corpus; when the copilot retrieves the poisoned record during triage it executes the embedded instructions (mis-classify, suppress, exfiltrate, or invoke defender tooling). The boundary is crossed because retrieved data is concatenated into the prompt without a data/instruction separation, and the corpus is writable by parties outside the trusted authoring set.
X-soc-rag-inject.exploitation_primitive: Operator files a support ticket / alert comment containing imperative instructions; when the SOC copilot retrieves it during triage, the agent obeys (e.g. reads a privileged table and posts it back, or closes the alert).

anchors.ref: Supabase MCP support-ticket prompt injection (Jul 2025)
anchors.note: Attacker-filed support ticket instructed a Cursor/Claude agent (service_role key) to SELECT integration_tokens and INSERT them into the visible ticket — writable-corpus retrieval injection in a defender-adjacent workflow

anchors.ref: Hidden-in-Plain-Text: Social-Web Indirect Prompt Injection in RAG (arXiv:2601.10923)
anchors.note: Benchmark for injection via user-writable web/social corpora consumed by RAG

X-soc-rag-inject.detection: Tag every retrieved chunk with author trust level and render low-trust corpus content as inert data (delimited, non-instruction) with attribution-gated answering; alert when a triage action (table read, alert close, tool call) is causally traced to a chunk authored by a non-analyst. Sanitize HTML/Markdown/Unicode in ticket text before embedding.

references.title: Supabase MCP can leak your entire SQL database — Simon Willison
Hidden-in-Plain-Text: A Benchmark for Social-Web Indirect Prompt Injection in RAG

X-memory-supplychain-pin.mechanism: An agent stores 'known-good' resolutions in long-term memory — a package name, a tool endpoint, an artifact URL it validated once — and on later sessions retrieves that memory and acts on it without re-validation. An attacker who can poison the memory (via query-only memory-injection or by influencing what the agent learns) plants a typosquatted package or attacker-controlled endpoint as the remembered-trusted resolution. The trust boundary is crossed across time: the original validation is not re-performed, so the supply-chain decision is laundered through the agent's own trusted history rather than a fresh integrity check.
X-memory-supplychain-pin.exploitation_primitive: Operator poisons the agent's memory so a future session resolves an attacker package/endpoint as the previously-validated 'known-good' entry and installs/calls it with no re-check.

anchors.ref: MINJA: Memory Injection Attack (Dong et al., NeurIPS 2025)
anchors.note: Query-only memory injection achieves >95% injection success, planting durable records the agent later treats as trusted history

anchors.ref: OWASP Agentic Security Initiative — ASI06 (Memory & Context Poisoning)
anchors.note: Recognizes persisted-memory poisoning as a top 2026 agentic risk

X-memory-supplychain-pin.detection: Treat remembered resolutions as cache, not authority: re-validate package signatures/endpoints against an external allowlist on every use rather than trusting stored 'known-good'. Alert when a memory-sourced identifier diverges from the canonical registry resolution, or when memory writes set durable tool/endpoint pins.

references.title: Memory Poisoning Attack and Defense on Memory-Based LLM-Agents (MINJA)
Memory poisoning in AI agents: exploits that wait — Christian Schneider

X-mcp-transport-desync.mechanism: MCP carries JSON-RPC 2.0 messages over stdio, HTTP+SSE, or Streamable HTTP, with messages delimited (newline-delimited, no embedded newlines; SSE events correlated by JSON-RPC `id`). If a broker/proxy and the backend disagree on framing — how message boundaries, `Content-Length`/chunked encoding, or SSE event delimiters are parsed — an attacker can smuggle a second tool call inside one apparent message, or split one call across the boundary, so the backend executes calls the broker never authorized. This is HTTP request-smuggling/desync reborn on the agent transport: the security control (the broker) and the executor (the backend) normalize the byte stream differently.
X-mcp-transport-desync.exploitation_primitive: Operator crafts an MCP HTTP/SSE payload whose framing the front proxy and backend parse differently, smuggling an extra `tools/call` past the broker's inspection.

anchors.ref: MCP Transports spec (2025-03-26)
anchors.note: Defines JSON-RPC framing, newline delimiting, and SSE id-correlation whose parser disagreements enable smuggling/desync

anchors.ref: MCP Transport: Architecture, Boundaries, and Failure Modes (pgEdge)
anchors.note: Analyzes transport boundary/framing failure modes across stdio/SSE/Streamable HTTP

X-mcp-transport-desync.detection: Normalize MCP traffic at a single authoritative parser; reject ambiguous framing (both Content-Length and chunked, embedded newlines in delimited messages, duplicated JSON-RPC ids). Log per-message tool-call counts and alert when backend-executed calls exceed broker-inspected calls for a connection.

references.title: Transports — Model Context Protocol
MCP Transport: Architecture, Boundaries, and Failure Modes

X-container-agent-rbac.mechanism: Kubernetes RBAC binds a ServiceAccount token (auto-mounted into the pod) to Roles/ClusterRoles, and that authority was typically scoped assuming a human runs `kubectl` deliberately and intermittently. An agent holding the SA executes cluster mutations autonomously at machine speed and scale; permissions that were 'fine for occasional human ops' (wildcards, broad ClusterRoleBindings, pod/exec, secret read) become a standing, continuously-exercisable capability. The boundary violation is not a new privilege but the mismatch between human-sized RBAC and an unattended actor that can chain those rights (e.g. read SA tokens, create privileged pods, edit bindings) to reach cluster-admin.
X-container-agent-rbac.exploitation_primitive: Operator drives the agent (via injection or rogue tasking) to use its mounted SA token to enumerate and chain over-broad RBAC — read secrets, create a privileged pod, or patch a ClusterRoleBinding — escalating autonomously.

anchors.ref: Unit 42 — Mitigating RBAC-Based Privilege Escalation in Kubernetes Platforms
anchors.note: Documents real RBAC escalation chains (token mounting, binding edits, privileged pods) that an autonomous SA-holder can exercise

anchors.ref: Security Considerations for Multi-agent Systems (arXiv:2603.09002)
anchors.note: Notes shared/over-broad ServiceAccounts let compromised agents modify cluster resources and bindings

X-container-agent-rbac.detection: Right-size agent ServiceAccounts to least privilege (no wildcards, no secret-read/exec/binding-edit unless required) and apply admission policy + rate limits on agent-originated mutations; alert on machine-speed bursts of API mutations, SA token reads, privileged pod creation, or ClusterRoleBinding edits from an agent identity.

references.title: Mitigating RBAC-Based Privilege Escalation in Popular Kubernetes Platforms — Unit 42
Security Considerations for Multi-agent Systems — arXiv:2603.09002

X-human-memory-toctou.mechanism: When a user issues a correction ('ignore that, it was wrong'), the agent honors it for the current turn (check time), but the original poisoned instruction may remain a durable record in long-term memory that is retrieved and replayed at a later use (use time). Because memory retrieval re-injects stored context as trusted history without reconciling it against the subsequent correction, user intent and stored state diverge across time — a check-vs-use (TOCTOU) gap in the memory layer. The correction edits the conversation, not the persisted memory entry, so the agent's effective belief reverts on the next session.
X-human-memory-toctou.exploitation_primitive: Operator plants a poisoned instruction into memory, lets the user 'correct' it (satisfying the in-session check), and relies on the unretracted memory entry being replayed as trusted context in a later session.

anchors.ref: MINJA: Memory Injection Attack (Dong et al., NeurIPS 2025)
anchors.note: Injected memory records persist and are replayed as the agent's own trusted history in later sessions, surviving in-conversation correction

anchors.ref: Memory Poisoning Attack and Defense on Memory-Based LLM-Agents (arXiv:2601.05504)
anchors.note: Characterizes durable poisoned memory that re-activates later — the use-time half of the divergence

X-human-memory-toctou.detection: Propagate corrections into the store: when a user retracts an instruction, tombstone or invalidate the corresponding memory record, not just the turn. Alert when retrieved memory conflicts with a more-recent user correction; surface provenance/timestamps so use-time can reconcile against check-time intent.

references.title: Memory Poisoning Attack and Defense on Memory-Based LLM-Agents
Memory & context poisoning: Don't let attackers rewrite your AI agent's memory — WorkOS

X-vectorstore-shared-index.mechanism: When multiple agents or apps share a single embedding index, retrieval eligibility is governed by vector similarity across the whole index unless hard per-consumer partitions exist. Without enforced partitioning, content written by agent A (its task context, fetched documents, intermediate notes) is indexed into the same space agent B searches, so B inherits A's write-context as read-context whenever similarity matches. The crossing point is shared retrieval scope: the index does not bind a chunk's read-audience to its writer, so multi-index trust carryover happens silently through normal top-k retrieval.
X-vectorstore-shared-index.exploitation_primitive: Operator (as agent A) writes content to the shared index tuned to match agent B's queries, causing B to retrieve and act on A-authored context as if it were its own grounded knowledge.

anchors.ref: Governed Shared Memory for Multi-Agent LLM Systems (arXiv:2606.24535)
anchors.note: Addresses cross-consumer carryover in shared multi-agent memory/index backends and the need for governed partitioning

anchors.ref: Giskard — Cross-session leak
anchors.note: Shows shared-index similarity collisions returning another consumer's content absent strict partitioning

X-vectorstore-shared-index.detection: Partition the index per consumer (separate namespaces/collections) and bind each chunk's read-audience to its writer; assert writer==reader scope on retrieval and alert on cross-consumer hits. Where a shared index is unavoidable, apply mandatory pre-filtering on consumer scope before the ANN search.

references.title: Governed Shared Memory for Multi-Agent LLM Systems — arXiv:2606.24535
Multi-Tenant Security and Isolation (AI system design guide)

X-cloud-memory-arn-confusion.mechanism: An agent remembers a cloud resource identifier (S3 bucket name, ARN, role name, CNAME target) and trusts it to denote the same resource on a later session. But many cloud namespaces are globally shared and re-registrable: a deleted bucket name or a dangling DNS/CNAME can be reclaimed by any account, and role/resource names can be re-created by a new owner. The provenance of a name is forgeable across time, so when the agent acts on the remembered name an attacker now controls, it reads from or writes to (or pulls binaries/templates from) the adversary's resource under the trust originally earned by the legitimate one.
X-cloud-memory-arn-confusion.exploitation_primitive: Operator reclaims an abandoned bucket name / dangling CNAME / re-creatable role that an agent has memorized as trusted, then serves attacker content to the agent's next remembered-name access.

anchors.ref: watchTowr — abandoned S3 bucket reclamation (2025)
anchors.note: Researchers re-registered ~150 abandoned S3 buckets once owned by governments/Fortune-500/vendors; over ~2 months they received 8M+ requests for software updates, binaries, and CloudFormation templates

anchors.ref: AWS — Threat tactic spotlight: Subdomain takeover
anchors.note: AWS documents dangling-DNS/globally-shared-namespace reclamation enabling name-confusion takeover

X-cloud-memory-arn-confusion.detection: Re-resolve and re-authorize remembered resource identifiers each session (verify bucket/role ownership, account id, and that CNAME targets still resolve to owned resources); alert on dangling DNS records and on remembered ARNs whose owning account changed. Prefer immutable resource IDs over re-registrable global names.

references.title: Threat tactic spotlight: Subdomain takeover — AWS Security Blog
How subdomain takeovers can punch holes in cloud data security — Proofpoint

GH1-rendered-ui-ground-truth.mechanism: A computer-use/browser agent builds its observation from a screenshot and/or the DOM accessibility (a11y) tree and treats that representation as ground truth about page state before acting. An attacker controls regions the agent's perception attends to but a human never visually parses the same way: off-viewport DOM nodes, CSS-hidden-but-a11y-exposed text (display:none, opacity:0, 1px font, white-on-white), near-invisible overlays, or adversarial pixel patches on a 'Confirm' control. The DOM/a11y label the agent reads diverges from the human-visible rendering, so the agent acts on a screen state that does not match the real consequence of clicking.
GH1-rendered-ui-ground-truth.exploitation_primitive: Operator embeds attacker text/controls in a11y-exposed-but-visually-hidden DOM (or an adversarial patch over a button) so the agent perceives and acts on a page state the human would never approve.

anchors.ref: Attacking Vision-Language Computer Agents via Pop-ups (arXiv:2411.02391)
anchors.note: Crafted overlays/pop-ups manipulate what the agent's vision model attends to, diverging from human perception

anchors.ref: Unit 42 — Web-based indirect prompt injection observed in the wild
anchors.note: Documents hidden-DOM injection (white-on-white, off-screen, hidden attributes) read by agents' text-extraction layer

GH1-rendered-ui-ground-truth.detection: Cross-check perception channels: reconcile rendered pixels against the a11y/DOM tree and flag elements that are a11y-exposed but visually hidden (display:none, opacity:0, off-viewport, sub-pixel fonts) or text outside the rendered viewport. Require human confirmation when the action target's visible label diverges from its DOM/accessible name.

references.title: Attacking Vision-Language Computer Agents via Pop-ups
Fooling AI Agents: Web-Based Indirect Prompt Injection Observed in the Wild — Unit 42

GH1-multimodal-metadata-injection.mechanism: Multimodal pipelines run a text-based safety/injection filter over the visible text and, once it passes, hand the image/audio/PDF object to the model as inert payload. But the model decodes channels the text filter never reads: EXIF/XMP fields, PDF annotation and form-field streams, ID3 tags, alt-text, glyph-substituted or steganographically/DCT-encoded text in images, and sub-audible audio. An injection carried in those out-of-band fields bypasses the guardrail and reaches the model's context, which extracts and may act on it. The boundary is crossed because the filter's coverage (visible text) is narrower than the model's decode surface (all embedded modalities/metadata).
GH1-multimodal-metadata-injection.exploitation_primitive: Operator embeds instructions in EXIF/XMP/PDF-annotation/ID3/alt-text or steganographic image layers of an artifact whose visible text is benign, so the payload passes the text filter and is decoded by the model.

anchors.ref: Invisible Injections: Steganographic Prompt Embedding in VLMs (arXiv:2507.22304)
anchors.note: Demonstrates VLMs extracting and executing instructions hidden via image steganography during normal processing

anchors.ref: Image-based Prompt Injection: Hijacking Multimodal LLMs (arXiv:2603.03637 / CSA research note)
anchors.note: Black-box embedding of adversarial instructions into images that override model behavior

GH1-multimodal-metadata-injection.detection: Strip/normalize all metadata (EXIF/XMP/ID3, PDF annotations and form fields) and re-encode images before model ingestion; run the injection/safety classifier over OCR'd image text, transcribed audio, and extracted document object streams — not just visible text. Alert on artifacts whose decoded/OCR content contains imperative instructions absent from the visible body.

references.title: Invisible Injections: Exploiting Vision-Language Models Through Steganographic Prompt Embedding
Multimodal prompt injection: attacks in images, audio, and video — Christian Schneider

GH1-tool-result-cache-poison.mechanism: Agent runtimes cache tool/function-call results (and semantic/LLM caches store prior responses) keyed by call signature or query embedding, then serve a cache hit as a faithful stand-in for re-executing the authenticated tool. An attacker who influences a tool's output once — a transient poisoned web fetch, a single malicious MCP response, or a crafted near-duplicate query that collides on the semantic cache key — gets that result frozen and re-served as authoritative across later runs, sessions, and other users, long after the upstream source is clean. The boundary is crossed because cache trust is inherited from the original (now-stale or attacker-influenced) execution and the cache is often shared across sessions.
GH1-tool-result-cache-poison.exploitation_primitive: Operator poisons a tool's output once (or seeds a colliding semantic-cache entry) so the frozen malicious result is served to subsequent agent runs and other users on cache hit.

anchors.ref: When Cache Poisoning Meets LLM Systems: Semantic Cache Poisoning (NDSS 2026)
anchors.note: Demonstrates injecting crafted cache entries so other users receive attacker-defined responses; reported 87-98% success against production LLM cache services

anchors.ref: Semantic Cache Poisoning: Corrupting the 'Fast Path'
anchors.note: Explains poison-once/serve-many distribution via shared semantic cache in agentic systems

GH1-tool-result-cache-poison.detection: Scope caches per-session/per-tenant or sign cache entries with the authenticated source; set short TTLs and revalidate high-impact tool results rather than trusting hits. Alert on semantic-cache hits with low key-to-query similarity (collision) and on cached results that diverge from a fresh re-execution sample.

references.title: When Cache Poisoning Meets LLM Systems — NDSS 2026
Semantic Cache Poisoning: Corrupting the 'Fast Path'

GH1-approval-fatigue-conditioning.mechanism: A human-in-the-loop confirmation gate assumes each approval is a fresh, independent act of judgment. But the agent's own high-frequency, near-identical benign confirmations behaviorally condition the operator into reflexive rubber-stamping — the same alert-fatigue/automation-bias dynamic SOCs have long known, relocated into the agent's inner loop. An attacker who can inject one malicious action into that stream gets it approved by a human whose 'consent' has been trained into a reflex by the legitimate workflow; the trust boundary (informed human judgment) is crossed not by defeating the gate but by exhausting the judgment it depends on.
GH1-approval-fatigue-conditioning.exploitation_primitive: Operator times a single malicious confirmation to ride a long run of benign, look-alike approvals so the conditioned operator reflexively approves it without reading.

anchors.ref: Approval Fatigue — Encyclopedia of Agentic Coding Patterns
anchors.note: Names and characterizes approval fatigue as alert-fatigue moved into the agent inner loop, collapsing true-positive catch rate

anchors.ref: Rubber Stamp Risk — when human oversight becomes false confidence
anchors.note: Documents rubber-stamping/automation bias degrading the value of human approval gates

GH1-approval-fatigue-conditioning.detection: Instrument approval latency and approve/deny ratios per operator; flag reflexive sign-offs (near-zero dwell time, 100% approve streaks). Inject honeypot/deliberately-wrong actions to catch rubber-stampers, require written rationale for high-impact approvals, batch and rate-limit low-risk confirmations so they don't condition reflexes, and rotate reviewers.

references.title: Approval Fatigue — Encyclopedia of Agentic Coding Patterns
Review Fatigue Is Breaking Human-in-the-Loop AI

GH1-eval-set-overfit-bypass.mechanism: Operators treat a passing safety eval / red-team benchmark score as a proxy for refusing the real-world class of behavior the eval represents. Because vendors optimize against the published benchmark, the model learns a 'pass-the-eval' shortcut rather than the underlying intent (Goodhart's law). An attacker crafts inputs that fall in the distribution gap between the finite eval set and the true intent — a distribution shift, spurious-shortcut, or contamination-adjacent region — so the input satisfies the model's learned shortcut (looks safe to the certified guardrail) while violating the spirit the eval claims to certify. The boundary is crossed because the metric, not the mission, was optimized.
GH1-eval-set-overfit-bypass.exploitation_primitive: Operator probes for and crafts inputs outside the published eval distribution (novel phrasing, composition, or attribute injection) that the benchmark-tuned guardrail passes but that elicit the prohibited behavior.

anchors.ref: Beyond Goodhart's Law: Dynamic Benchmark for Multi-Agent Compliance (arXiv:2606.07805)
anchors.note: Shows static benchmark optimization yields strategic rule-violation ('Machiavellian') behavior — a direct Goodhart manifestation

anchors.ref: Goodhart's Law Applies to NLP's Explanation Benchmarks (arXiv:2308.14272)
anchors.note: Empirical evidence that optimizing a benchmark diverges from the property it intends to measure

GH1-eval-set-overfit-bypass.detection: Use dynamic/rotating red-team suites and frozen holdouts rather than a static published eval; monitor for guardrail decisions that diverge between the certified eval distribution and live traffic. Track guardrail KPIs on out-of-distribution and compositional inputs, and stack multiple independent metrics to resist single-metric gaming.

references.title: Beyond Goodhart's Law: A Dynamic Benchmark for Multi-Agent Compliance
Closing the Eval–Deployment Gap in AI Systems

GH1-lora-adapter-provenance.mechanism: Loading a downloaded fine-tune or LoRA/adapter on top of a vetted base model composes new weights but assumes the adapter is a benign behavioral delta, so the composition inherits the base model's safety expectations and lineage. A small, cheaply-distributed adapter can encode a backdoor or alignment-stripping behavior that activates only on attacker-chosen triggers (or only when a colluding set of individually-benign adapters is combined), silently overriding the base model's safety while presenting as the trusted base. The boundary is crossed at composition: the safety review evaluated the base (or each adapter in isolation), but the deployed behavior is the merged weights, which no isolated check covered.
GH1-lora-adapter-provenance.exploitation_primitive: Operator publishes a backdoored/alignment-stripping LoRA to a model hub; a deployment loads it on a trusted base and runs it with base-model safety assumptions, with the trigger firing only on chosen inputs.

anchors.ref: Detecting Backdoored LoRAs from Weights Alone (arXiv:2602.15195)
anchors.note: Confirms supply-chain backdoored adapters distributed via hubs that behave normally except on triggers

anchors.ref: Colluding LoRA: Composite Fine-Tuning Attacks on LLM Safety (CSA / arXiv:2603.12681)
anchors.note: Shows adapters benign in isolation that strip safety alignment when combined, defeating isolated review

GH1-lora-adapter-provenance.detection: Verify adapter provenance (signature/publisher) and run weight-level backdoor screening (trigger-agnostic analysis of adapter weight matrices) before deployment; re-run the full safety eval on the composed model, not just the base, and on any multi-adapter merge. Alert on adapters from unverified publishers or with anomalous weight statistics.

references.title: Detecting Backdoored LoRAs from Weights Alone — arXiv:2602.15195
Colluding LoRA: A Composite Attack on LLM Safety Alignment

GH1-dynamic-client-registration-abuse.mechanism: OAuth 2.1 authorization servers supporting Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591) let MCP/agent clients self-register and then ride a consent flow; the AS treats successful registration plus user consent as adequate provenance for a client identity. An attacker stands up a rogue MCP server or agent client that self-registers with a benign name/logo (RFC 7591 explicitly warns a rogue client may impersonate a legitimate client's name/logo/software-id) and rides the user's habituated consent or a manipulated agent into granting scoped tokens. An unvetted, ephemeral client thereby obtains durable delegated authority with no human-recognizable app behind it; open registration endpoints also enable client impersonation and registration flooding.
GH1-dynamic-client-registration-abuse.exploitation_primitive: Operator self-registers a rogue OAuth client via DCR with a trusted-looking name/logo and steers the agent/user through consent to mint scoped, durable delegated tokens.

anchors.ref: RFC 7591 — OAuth 2.0 Dynamic Client Registration (security considerations)
anchors.note: Spec itself warns a rogue client may use a legitimate client's name/logo/software-id and require redirect-URI registration to mitigate

anchors.ref: Obsidian Security — When MCP Meets OAuth: one-click account takeover
anchors.note: Documents DCR/OAuth pitfalls in MCP enabling rogue-client account takeover

GH1-dynamic-client-registration-abuse.detection: Gate DCR behind allowlists/attestation and software-statement verification; rate-limit and monitor registration endpoints for flooding and lookalike client names/logos. Alert on newly-registered clients requesting broad scopes, and surface human-verifiable app identity (verified publisher) in consent screens rather than self-asserted name/logo.

references.title: RFC 7591: OAuth 2.0 Dynamic Client Registration Protocol
Tips to Harden OAuth Dynamic Client Registration in MCP Servers — Descope

GH1-prompt-cache-timing-leak.mechanism: Multi-tenant LLM serving shares a prefix/prompt (KV) cache across requests for efficiency, treating cache hits as a private latency optimization. But automatic prefix caching makes time-to-first-token (TTFT) depend on whether a request's prefix is already cached, so TTFT carries information about other tenants'/sessions' prompt contents. An attacker submits candidate prefixes and measures TTFT to detect a cache hit (prefix already present from a privileged session), then recursively extends the matched prefix token-by-token, recovering secret prompt content (system prompts, document fragments, PII) through the shared-cache timing side channel.
GH1-prompt-cache-timing-leak.exploitation_primitive: Operator issues crafted prefix probes and times TTFT to distinguish cache hit vs miss, pruning and extending the prefix to reconstruct another tenant's secret prompt content.

anchors.ref: The Early Bird Catches the Leak: Timing Side Channels in LLM Serving (arXiv:2409.20002)
anchors.note: Demonstrates TTFT-based prefix-cache hit/miss detection recovering prompt content in shared serving

anchors.ref: I Know What You Asked: Prompt Leakage via KV-Cache Sharing (NDSS 2025)
anchors.note: Recovers system prompts token-by-token via shared KV-cache timing (~99% per-token hit/miss detection)

GH1-prompt-cache-timing-leak.detection: Disable cross-tenant/cross-domain prefix-cache sharing (scope caches per security domain) or add timing defenses (constant-time-to-first-token padding, per-tenant cache partitions). Detect probing by alerting on high-volume, low-diversity prefix queries from one client and on TTFT-correlated query sequences.

references.title: The Early Bird Catches the Leak — arXiv:2409.20002
CacheSolidarity: Preventing Prefix Caching Side Channels in Multi-tenant LLM Serving

GH1-interpreter-egress-trust.mechanism: A code-interpreter/sandbox is built to contain execution (filesystem, CPU, process isolation) and treats any permitted network egress (package installs, API fetches, DNS) as a benign convenience rather than a confidentiality boundary. Prompt-injected or attacker-influenced content drives the agent to emit code that runs in the sandbox and uses that permitted egress to exfiltrate the agent's context, secrets, or fetched documents. The boundary is crossed because containment ignored egress: even a 'no network' sandbox often still resolves DNS, so data can be base64-encoded into subdomain queries and leaked out-of-band while the agent returns a benign-looking response.
GH1-interpreter-egress-trust.exploitation_primitive: Operator injects content that makes the agent generate sandbox code which encodes context/secrets into an outbound fetch or DNS query to an attacker domain.

anchors.ref: BeyondTrust — Pwning AI Code Interpreters in AWS Bedrock AgentCore
anchors.note: Shows DNS queries egress a 'no network' sandbox; output base64-encoded into subdomain queries for data extraction

anchors.ref: Silent Egress (arXiv:2602.22450)
anchors.note: Implicit prompt injection induces unintended network egress (~89% probability) while the assistant returns a benign response

GH1-interpreter-egress-trust.detection: Apply deny-by-default egress with explicit domain allowlists for sandboxes (including DNS); log and alert on outbound connections/DNS to non-allowlisted destinations and on high-entropy subdomain labels (DNS exfil). Treat sandbox egress as a confidentiality boundary, not just an execution-containment afterthought.

references.title: Pwning AI Code Interpreters in AWS Bedrock AgentCore — BeyondTrust
Silent Egress: When Implicit Prompt Injection Makes LLM Agents Leak — arXiv:2602.22450

GH1-system-prompt-extraction-targeting.mechanism: An agent deployment treats its system prompt, tool schemas, and guardrail instructions as a confidential control layer, designing injection defenses on the assumption the attacker operates blind to the exact wording and tool surface. The attacker first extracts that hidden layer — via leakage prompts, refusal-boundary probing, error messages, encoding tricks (Base64/ROT13/leetspeak), or write-primitive reconstruction (e.g. forcing it into form-field values) — disclosing the literal instruction wording and tool names. They then craft an injection that precisely targets the disclosed instructions (overriding the exact phrasing) and invokes the exact tool names/parameters, collapsing the security-by-obscurity gap the defenses silently depended on.
GH1-system-prompt-extraction-targeting.exploitation_primitive: Operator runs extraction prompts to recover the system prompt and tool schema, then writes an injection tailored to the disclosed wording and tool names to reliably override guardrails.

anchors.ref: OWASP LLM07:2025 — System Prompt Leakage
anchors.note: Recognizes system-prompt/tool-config disclosure as a top LLM risk that exposes the toolchain, endpoints, and rules an attacker can then target

anchors.ref: Praetorian — System Prompt Extraction via LLM Write Primitives
anchors.note: Extracts system prompts even when chat output is locked down, by exploiting form-field write primitives

GH1-system-prompt-extraction-targeting.detection: Monitor for extraction-style inputs (asking to repeat/print instructions, encoded variants, refusal-boundary probing) and for outputs that echo system-prompt or tool-schema text; treat any system-prompt leakage as a defense-collapse event. Do not rely on prompt secrecy for security — enforce tool-call authorization independent of prompt wording.

references.title: Understanding LLM07: System Prompt Leakage — Keysight
Exploiting LLM Write Primitives: System Prompt Extraction — Praetorian

GH1-subagent-context-inheritance.mechanism: When a parent agent spawns a sub-agent it passes along task context, assuming that context is sanitized internal state; the sub-agent treats inherited instructions and data as trusted parent tasking. But untrusted content the parent absorbed (a poisoned document, web result, or prior injection) rides into the spawned sub-agent's context unfiltered, and the sub-agent — often holding different or broader tools — executes attacker intent as authentic parent direction. The boundary violation is the absence of re-validation at the spawn: inter-agent communication is implicitly trusted and unsanitized, so a local injection in the parent propagates and is structurally amplified across the agent boundary.
GH1-subagent-context-inheritance.exploitation_primitive: Operator gets a prompt-injection into the parent's context (via a fetched doc/web result) and relies on the parent spawning a sub-agent that inherits and acts on it with the sub-agent's tool authority.

anchors.ref: When Child Inherits: Modeling and Exploiting Subagent Spawn (arXiv:2605.08460)
anchors.note: Models insecure memory/context inheritance letting a local compromise spread across the spawn boundary into sub-agents

anchors.ref: Multi-Agent AI Security (Augment Code)
anchors.note: Notes injections amplify because inter-agent communication is trusted, unfiltered, and trust hierarchies are implicit

GH1-subagent-context-inheritance.detection: Re-validate inherited context at the spawn boundary: re-run injection/safety classification on data passed parent->child and strip untrusted-origin instructions; tag context with provenance/trust so sub-agents can distinguish parent tasking from absorbed external content. Alert when a sub-agent's high-impact tool call traces to externally-sourced inherited text.

references.title: When Child Inherits: Modeling and Exploiting Subagent Spawn in Multi-Agent Networks
Multi-Agent AI Security: Enterprise Risks, Compliance, and Mitigation — Augment Code

GH1-rag-citation-spoof.mechanism: A RAG/agentic-search system presents citations as verifiable provenance, so the human consumer trusts that a cited link substantiates the claim and that the agent grounded the claim in that real source. The agent instead emits a fabricated citation (a plausible but nonexistent source), a real URL that does not actually support the claim, or cites an attacker-planted document crafted to look authoritative; the human accepts the unverified claim precisely because it appears cited. The trust signal (provenance) is decoupled from the actual support relationship, so false or attacker-chosen content is laundered through the appearance of a citation — and web 'verification' can compound it when other pages cite the same ghost reference.
GH1-rag-citation-spoof.exploitation_primitive: Operator induces the agent to attach an authoritative-looking citation (fabricated, mismatched, or pointing to attacker-planted content) to an unsupported claim so the human accepts it as grounded.

anchors.ref: FACTUM: Mechanistic Detection of Citation Hallucination in Long-Form RAG (arXiv:2601.05866)
anchors.note: Establishes citation hallucination (misattribution to incorrect/fabricated sources) as a distinct RAG failure

anchors.ref: Detecting and Correcting Reference Hallucinations in Commercial LLMs and Deep Research Agents (arXiv:2604.03173)
anchors.note: Measures fabricated/mismatched references in production LLMs and research agents

GH1-rag-citation-spoof.detection: Verify citations programmatically before display: confirm the URL/source exists and that the cited passage actually entails the claim (claim->evidence matching / attribution-gated answering); flag claims whose cited source fails entailment or resolves to attacker-writable content. Surface a verification status next to each citation rather than presenting bare links as provenance.

references.title: FACTUM: Mechanistic Detection of Citation Hallucination in Long-Form RAG
Detecting and Correcting Reference Hallucinations in Commercial LLMs and Deep Research Agents

GH3-agent-hmi-operator-impersonation.mechanism: HMI/SCADA front-ends issue control writes (e.g., Modbus write-single-register/coil FC05/FC06/FC16, OPC-UA Write service, DNP3 operate) the moment an operator clicks; the control authority is bound to the console session, not to any cryptographic proof of human deliberation. When an LLM copilot is wired into the same console session and is permitted to emit those same writes, the PLC/RTU cannot distinguish an agent-generated setpoint change from a trained operator's because legacy OT protocols carry no per-command actor identity or intent attestation. The agent's prompt-injected or hallucinated 'recommendation' therefore actuates at machine speed with full operator authority.
GH3-agent-hmi-operator-impersonation.exploitation_primitive: Indirect prompt injection of the copilot's input context (alarm text, log entry, work-order note) that the agent translates into a console-authorized setpoint/command write, bypassing the human deliberation the OT authority model assumes.

anchors.ref: Oldsmar, FL water treatment HMI setpoint tamper (2021)
anchors.note: Remote actor used HMI access to raise sodium hydroxide setpoint ~100x; illustrates that a console-originated setpoint write actuates the physical process with no independent safety judgment, the exact trust the agent inherits.

anchors.ref: MITRE ATT&CK for ICS T0831 Manipulation of Control / T0855 Unauthorized Command Message
anchors.note: Catalogs adversary manipulation of control via command messages a controller treats as legitimate operator action.

GH3-agent-hmi-operator-impersonation.detection: Behavioral baselining of HMI-originated writes: flag setpoint/command writes whose inter-command timing, value step-size, or sequence is inconsistent with human operator pacing; correlate console session command logs with copilot tool-call audit trail and require dual-channel confirmation for safety-relevant writes.

references.title: CISA AA21-042A — Compromise of U.S. Water Treatment Facility (Oldsmar)
MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — T0831 Manipulation of Control

GH3-agent-protocol-bridge-approval-loss.mechanism: An NL-to-fieldbus agent decomposes one approved high-level intent into many wire-level writes (Modbus FC05/06/16, OPC-UA Write/Method-Call, DNP3 operate). The human approval gate binds to the natural-language request, but the field protocols themselves carry no authorization token, signature, or scope on individual function codes — most are insecure-by-design with plaintext, unauthenticated engineering operations. So sub-steps that were injected or over-broadly expanded reach the controller carrying 'inherited' approval the operator never granted to those specific writes.
GH3-agent-protocol-bridge-approval-loss.exploitation_primitive: Goal-decomposition expansion or injected sub-step that emits function codes outside the scope the human actually approved, exploiting that the approval was never re-checked at the function-code boundary.

anchors.ref: Forescout OT:ICEFALL (2022)
anchors.note: 56 insecure-by-design flaws across 10 OT vendors; documents that engineering protocols (Modbus, OPC-UA variants, proprietary) accept controller/firmware operations with broken or absent authentication, so a wire-level write needs no per-command approval.

anchors.ref: MITRE ATT&CK for ICS T0855 Unauthorized Command Message / T0836 Modify Parameter
anchors.note: Adversary sends command/parameter-modify messages a controller accepts as authorized engineering action.

GH3-agent-protocol-bridge-approval-loss.detection: Per-function-code authorization mapping at the bridge: enforce an allow-list of {approved intent -> permitted function codes / register ranges} and alert on any emitted write outside the bound scope of the approving request; log the NL-intent-to-functioncode expansion for review.

references.title: Forescout — OT:ICEFALL: 56 Vulnerabilities Caused by Insecure-by-Design Practices in OT
MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — T0836 Modify Parameter

GH3-agent-fleet-telematics-to-can.mechanism: Fleet-telematics backends expose remote-actuation APIs (door unlock, immobilizer/fuel cutoff, remote start) that a TCU/gateway accepts as backend-signed dispatcher intent and relays onto the vehicle CAN bus. Many such platforms have weak or hard-coded authentication and unauthenticated command channels, so an authenticated-but-manipulated dispatcher agent can issue bulk commands the backend forwards verbatim. The vehicle gateway translates these into CAN-level actuation with no human-need verification.
GH3-agent-fleet-telematics-to-can.exploitation_primitive: Injected task text (poisoned support ticket, malicious route note) steers the dispatcher-scoped agent into emitting bulk remote-actuation API calls that the telematics backend relays to vehicles as authentic dispatcher intent.

anchors.ref: CVE-2022-2107 / CVE-2022-2141 (MiCODUS MV720 GPS tracker)
anchors.note: Hard-coded master password and broken authentication let a remote attacker send SMS commands including fuel cutoff to ~1.5M trackers across fleets; CVSS 9.8. Demonstrates telematics backends actuating vehicles on forwarded commands without verifying real-world need.

anchors.ref: MITRE ATT&CK T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application
anchors.note: Fleet/telematics backend is the exploitable public-facing application bridging agent intent to physical actuation.

GH3-agent-fleet-telematics-to-can.detection: Rate-limit and anomaly-score remote-actuation API calls per dispatcher identity; alert on bulk/fan-out actuation (many VINs in a short window), geographically implausible command targets, and immobilizer/fuel-cutoff commands lacking a linked dispatcher ticket; require step-up auth for safety-impacting fleet-wide commands.

references.title: CISA ICS Advisory ICSA-22-200-01 — MiCODUS MV720 GPS Tracker
TechCrunch — Security flaws in GPS tracker exposing 1M vehicle locations

GH3-agent-mdm-mass-wipe-policy.mechanism: MDM/UEM platforms (Intune, Jamf, Mobile Guardian) execute remote wipe, configuration-profile push, and trust-CA installation against any device set on the authority of an admin-scoped token via the platform API. The authority model assumes a human admin's pace; there is no blast-radius constraint distinguishing a single targeted action from an estate-wide one. An LLM IT-ops agent holding that token executes the same privileged API calls, so prompt-injected instructions push malicious profiles or trigger mass wipe that the platform treats as authentic admin intent.
GH3-agent-mdm-mass-wipe-policy.exploitation_primitive: Prompt injection via a helpdesk message or device-name string the agent reads, causing it to invoke the MDM wipe/profile-push API across the enrolled estate with the admin token's full authority.

anchors.ref: Mobile Guardian mass remote wipe (Aug 2024)
anchors.note: Attackers gained unauthorized platform access and remotely wiped thousands of student devices, demonstrating MDM remote-wipe weaponization with no blast-radius limit.

anchors.ref: Stryker / Microsoft Intune mass-wipe claim (2026)
anchors.note: Threat actor compromised Intune admin credentials and used built-in remote wipe to factory-reset enrolled devices; attack lived entirely in cloud audit logs (Entra/Intune), invisible to endpoint EDR.

GH3-agent-mdm-mass-wipe-policy.detection: Monitor MDM/UEM cloud audit logs (Entra ID sign-in, Intune audit) for wipe/profile-push/CA-install actions exceeding a per-window device-count threshold; require human approval and rate-limits for bulk destructive actions; alert on new trust-CA or configuration-profile deployments to large device groups.

references.title: SecurityWeek — Thousands of Devices Wiped Remotely Following Mobile Guardian Hack
GovInfoSecurity — Microsoft Intune MDM Gains Notoriety After Stryker Hack

GH3-audio-channel-injection-into-vehicle.mechanism: Voice agents treat any audio their microphone decodes as an authenticated owner utterance and forward the transcribed text into the LLM-tool loop with standing device authority. DolphinAttack exploits the nonlinearity of MEMS microphone front-ends: voice commands amplitude-modulated onto an ultrasonic (>20 kHz) carrier are inaudible to humans but get demodulated back to baseband audio inside the mic circuit and transcribed as a real command. The perception boundary has no liveness/authenticity check, so injected inaudible audio drives actuation as if spoken by the owner.
GH3-audio-channel-injection-into-vehicle.exploitation_primitive: Inaudible ultrasonic-carrier (or RF-replayed) audio injected near the device that the microphone demodulates and the agent transcribes into owner-authority commands the human never uttered.

anchors.ref: DolphinAttack: Inaudible Voice Commands (Zhang et al., ACM CCS 2017)
anchors.note: Demonstrated inaudible ultrasonic command injection against Siri, Google Now, Alexa, Cortana, S Voice, HiVoice; PoC included manipulating an Audi navigation system, directly showing vehicle/agent actuation from injected audio.

anchors.ref: Sirens' Whisper: Inaudible Near-Ultrasonic Jailbreaks of Speech-Driven LLMs (2026)
anchors.note: Extends inaudible-audio injection to modern speech-driven LLM agents, the exact agent-loop target.

GH3-audio-channel-injection-into-vehicle.detection: Microphone-side ultrasonic/AM artifact detection (energy in suppressed high-frequency bands, demodulation residue); SVM-based classification of legitimate vs. modulated audio; speaker liveness/verification and out-of-band confirmation before safety-relevant actuation (unlock, start, garage).

references.title: DolphinAttack: Inaudible Voice Commands (arXiv:1708.09537)
USSLab — DolphinAttack project page

GH3-agent-rf-presence-ground-truth.mechanism: Agents consuming RFID/badge/BLE/Wi-Fi telemetry treat the signal as ground-truth proof of a named human's physical presence. Low-frequency 125 kHz RFID badges transmit a static, unencrypted UID that is trivially cloned; BLE passive-entry tokens can be relayed end-to-end with sub-millisecond added latency because the protocol has no distance-bounding. Because the presence signal carries no proof-of-proximity or anti-relay binding, a cloned UID or relayed token is ingested as a verified identity-and-location fact on which the agent grants access or escalates context.
GH3-agent-rf-presence-ground-truth.exploitation_primitive: Clone a static badge UID or operate a two-device BLE relay so the agent ingests a forged 'person X is physically here' signal and unlocks/approves co-located actions.

anchors.ref: NCC Group — Tesla BLE Phone-as-a-Key Relay Attack (2022)
anchors.note: Layer-link BLE relay unlocked and drove a Tesla Model 3 with the paired phone ~25 m away, proving relayed RF presence is accepted as legitimate proximity by the trusting system.

anchors.ref: RFID badge cloning of 125 kHz / unencrypted credentials
anchors.note: Static-UID low-frequency badges are cloned from proximity and replayed to defeat physical access control.

GH3-agent-rf-presence-ground-truth.detection: Require anti-relay controls (BLE distance-bounding / RTT, UWB ranging) and challenge-response credentials rather than static UIDs; correlate presence signal with an independent channel (camera, PIN, mobile attestation); alert on impossible-travel or simultaneous multi-location presence for the same identity.

references.title: NCC Group — Technical Advisory: Tesla BLE Phone-as-a-Key Vulnerable to Relay Attacks
Keysight — Understanding RFID Technology and Its Security Implications

GH3-agent-ota-self-verified-firmware.mechanism: Secure firmware update relies on the device cryptographically verifying an image signature and enforcing monotonic version/rollback counters before flashing. When an AI fleet-ops agent selects and pushes images, the human-in-the-loop substitutes the agent's narrated 'I verified the signature/changelog' for an actual signature check. If the device or pipeline does not independently enforce signature + anti-rollback (a common insecure-by-design gap), the agent can push an unsigned, swapped, or downgraded image — and a still-validly-signed older vulnerable image passes verification, enabling firmware-level persistence at fleet scale.
GH3-agent-ota-self-verified-firmware.exploitation_primitive: Agent reads an attacker-supplied changelog/release page, rationalizes a swapped or downgraded image as legitimate, and pushes it to the fleet, standing in for a cryptographic verification that was never enforced.

anchors.ref: Forescout OT:ICEFALL (2022) — insecure firmware update category
anchors.note: 21% of the 56 flaws were firmware-manipulation issues: missing or broken signature verification in OT firmware update mechanisms.

anchors.ref: CVE-2023-24932 (Secure Boot downgrade, BlackLotus)
anchors.note: Downgrade attack loads an older still-Microsoft-signed boot manager that Secure Boot trusts, illustrating that a validly-signed older image defeats provenance assumptions absent anti-rollback.

GH3-agent-ota-self-verified-firmware.detection: Enforce device-side signature verification and monotonic rollback counters independent of the agent; require code-signing of the deployed artifact hash and compare against an attested SBOM; alert on version regressions and any image whose signer/hash is not on the trusted release allow-list; treat agent 'verification' narration as non-authoritative.

references.title: Forescout — OT:ICEFALL research labs page
NVD — CVE-2023-24932 (Secure Boot security feature bypass)

GH3-agent-confused-deputy-ca-issuance.mechanism: PKI issuance (ACME, internal CA, cloud private CA) binds a name to a key after domain-control validation (HTTP-01/DNS-01) or an internal authorization policy, and treats a requester holding issuance authority as having validated control. An agent with issuance/approval scope that is prompt-injected becomes a confused deputy: it requests or approves certificates for names it does not legitimately control, or installs a trust anchor, using the CA's own authority. Historic DCV-bypass misissuance (subdomain-to-base-domain, unprivileged-port validation) shows the validation boundary is exploitable even without an agent.
GH3-agent-confused-deputy-ca-issuance.exploitation_primitive: Prompt injection via a ticket, domain string, or service descriptor the PKI-ops agent reads, causing it to issue/approve a certificate (or install a CA) for an attacker-chosen name with the pipeline's authority.

anchors.ref: WoSign DCV bypass misissuance / SSL.com MX-hostname DCV bypass (Mozilla Bug 1961406)
anchors.note: Real CA misissuance from validation flaws (subdomain-to-base-domain, MX-hostname DCV bypass) — the confused-deputy outcome an injected agent can reproduce.

anchors.ref: MITRE ATT&CK T1649 Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates
anchors.note: Adversary obtaining certificates to impersonate identities / undermine trust.

GH3-agent-confused-deputy-ca-issuance.detection: Certificate Transparency log monitoring for unexpected issuance of names you own; alert on agent-initiated issuance/approval lacking an independently validated control proof; constrain agent issuance scope with CA name-constraints and require human approval for trust-anchor installation or wildcard/high-value names.

references.title: Mozilla Bugzilla 1961406 — SSL.com DCV bypass / fake certificates for MX hostnames
SSLMate — Timeline of Certificate Authority Failures

GH3-poisoned-sensor-into-ai-actuation-mo.mechanism: An AI anomaly/control model ingests wireless-sensor telemetry as a faithful representation of physical state and bases actuation on its inference. Because legacy ICS sensor protocols (e.g., Modbus) lack authentication and integrity, an attacker can inject false readings, slow bias drift, or replayed 'normal' data. The model then infers a benign or inverted state and either actuates a harmful change or suppresses a needed protective action — a false-data-injection attack weaponizing the model's data-to-control coupling, since the inference layer cannot distinguish authentic physics from spoofed telemetry.
GH3-poisoned-sensor-into-ai-actuation-mo.exploitation_primitive: False-data-injection / bias-drift / replay on the unauthenticated sensor feed that moves the model's inference across an actuation threshold (or masks an alarm condition).

anchors.ref: Stealthy False Command/Data Injection on Modbus-based SCADA
anchors.note: Modbus's lack of authentication lets an attacker inject crafted packets carrying false sensor/command data that the control loop accepts as real.

anchors.ref: MITRE ATT&CK for ICS T0856 Spoof Reporting Message / T0832 Manipulation of View
anchors.note: Adversary spoofs sensor/reporting messages so operators or automation perceive a false process state.

GH3-poisoned-sensor-into-ai-actuation-mo.detection: Physics-based / process-invariant anomaly detection and sensor-fingerprinting (e.g., hardware noise fingerprints) to flag telemetry inconsistent with physical dynamics; cross-validate redundant sensors; alert on flat-line/replayed values, impossible rate-of-change, and divergence between sensor stream and actuator-response model.
