GH2-jit-pam-approval-workflow-abuse.detection: Alert on elevations granted via auto-approval policy, on approver==requester or close approver-overlap, and on a high rate of just-under-threshold justification reuse; record and review session activity against the stated justification; flag JIT grants that are re-requested back-to-back to simulate standing access; require out-of-band approval for the most privileged scopes.

references.title: ManageEngine PAM360 - JIT privilege elevation / auto-approval
Delinea - What is Just-in-Time Access

GH9-saas-oauth-transitive-scope-toxic-ch.mechanism: A SaaS tenant evaluates each third-party OAuth grant in isolation ('App A may read my CRM') and never reasons about the transitive capability graph where App A's read scope composes with App B's external-write/outbound scope into a read-then-exfiltrate primitive neither grant alone implies. Each app holds long-lived OAuth access/refresh tokens; once one low-sensitivity integration (or its token) is compromised, the attacker reads sensitive records through App A and pushes them out through App B's sanctioned outbound scope. Because every hop uses a valid, tenant-sanctioned token, the lateral movement is indistinguishable from normal SaaS-to-SaaS automation traffic, and no single consent screen ever showed the combined authority.
GH9-saas-oauth-transitive-scope-toxic-ch.exploitation_primitive: Compromise one low-privilege integration token, enumerate the tenant's other sanctioned OAuth grants, and chain a read scope on App A with an outbound/write scope on App B to exfiltrate data using only valid tokens, no new consent and no credential theft.
GH9-saas-oauth-transitive-scope-toxic-ch.mitre_gap: Closest are T1550.001 (Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token) and T1199 (Trusted Relationship), but each models a single token/relationship. ATT&CK has no technique for OAuth transitive-scope toxic combinations, the composition of independently-granted scopes across multiple sanctioned apps into a capability exceeding any one consent; T1550.001 describes using one token, not chaining the scope graph.

anchors.ref: Salesloft Drift OAuth token compromise (UNC6395), Aug 2025
anchors.note: Compromised Drift OAuth tokens were used to authenticate to 700+ orgs' Salesforce instances and mass-exfiltrate data (AWS keys, Snowflake tokens); shows long-lived sanctioned OAuth tokens enabling SaaS-to-SaaS lateral data theft.

anchors.ref: AppOmni - Drift breach (UNC6395): why Salesforce OAuth integrations are a growing risk
anchors.note: Analysis of how sanctioned OAuth integration scopes become an exfiltration path indistinguishable from normal automation.

GH9-saas-oauth-transitive-scope-toxic-ch.detection: Build a tenant-wide OAuth grant inventory and compute the transitive scope graph to flag toxic read+external-write combinations; alert on OAuth tokens performing bulk reads followed by outbound transfers, on first-seen app-to-app data flows, and on refresh-token use from new IPs/ASNs; cap token lifetime and require periodic re-consent for high-scope apps.

references.title: Google Cloud (Mandiant) - Data theft via Salesloft Drift (UNC6395)
AppOmni - Drift breach (UNC6395) analysis

GH9-inbound-webhook-as-control-plane.mechanism: A SaaS app that exposes an inbound webhook treats the event payload ('payment.succeeded', 'user.verified', 'document.signed') as an authoritative statement of what happened in a partner system and lets it directly mutate state, rather than re-verifying against the source of truth. The exploitable conditions: endpoints with no signature; verifiers that parse-then-verify a re-canonicalized body (signature computed over different bytes than those acted on); HMAC schemes with no timestamp/nonce binding (enabling replay); and the empty-secret defect where an unset HMAC key yields a publicly computable signature. In all cases attacker-supplied data crosses into the application's control plane, flipping entitlements, marking orders paid, or promoting accounts, without the receiver pulling current state back from the originator.
GH9-inbound-webhook-as-control-plane.exploitation_primitive: POST a forged or replayed event (exploiting a missing/empty signing secret, or a verifier that signs the raw body but acts on a re-parsed one) to flip a state-changing flag, e.g. credit quota/mark-paid/promote-account, directly via the webhook.
GH9-inbound-webhook-as-control-plane.mitre_gap: Closest are T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1565.001 (Data Manipulation: Stored Data Manipulation), but neither captures webhook-as-control-plane, where an untrusted inbound assertion is wired directly to a state mutation absent re-verification. ATT&CK has no technique for forged/replayed webhook events as a first-class control-plane abuse; T1190 implies a code-level exploit, whereas this is trusted-by-design event ingestion.

anchors.ref: CVE-2026-41432 (QuantumNous/new-api < 0.12.10)
anchors.note: Empty StripeWebhookSecret default lets an attacker compute valid signatures and forge payment webhooks to credit arbitrary quota without payment; concrete webhook-as-control-plane forgery.

anchors.ref: Stripe webhook signature/timestamp tolerance docs
anchors.note: Documents the timestamp-in-signature and 5-minute tolerance that prevent replay; absence of these checks is the abuse condition.

GH9-inbound-webhook-as-control-plane.detection: Reject webhooks lacking a configured signing secret (fail closed on empty secret); verify HMAC over the exact raw bytes acted upon and enforce a timestamp-tolerance + nonce/idempotency-key to block replay; for any value-releasing event, re-fetch authoritative state from the originating API before mutating; alert on webhook events that drive entitlement/financial state changes without a matching origin-API confirmation.

references.title: GHSA-xff3-5c9p-2mr4 / CVE-2026-41432 - Stripe webhook signature bypass via empty secret
Stripe - Receive Stripe events in your webhook endpoint

GH9-ipaas-shared-connection-privilege-fa.mechanism: iPaaS platforms (Zapier/Make/Workato) let any recipe/scenario in a workspace execute under the workspace's shared, pre-authorized connections, so a flow author inherits the full authority of every connection an admin once added, dozens of long-lived OAuth grants to business-critical systems, without the platform re-checking that this particular automation/author/trigger was authorized for that specific connection's scope. The stored OAuth tokens become an ambient capability any flow can borrow. One workspace compromise (a low-privilege builder account, an over-shared workspace, or a malicious imported template) inherits the union of every connected SaaS at once, because attaching an existing connection to a new flow requires no fresh consent.
GH9-ipaas-shared-connection-privilege-fa.exploitation_primitive: With author/edit rights in the workspace, create or import a flow that attaches an existing privileged shared connection and use it to pivot into systems you were never individually granted, the connection's stored tokens execute the flow with the owner's authority.
GH9-ipaas-shared-connection-privilege-fa.mitre_gap: Closest are T1078.004 (Valid Accounts: Cloud) and T1199 (Trusted Relationship), but neither models iPaaS shared-connection authority, the platform-mediated borrowing of one principal's stored OAuth grant by an unrelated automation. ATT&CK has no technique for shared-connection privilege fanout where the automation layer re-uses ambient credentials across flows; T1078.004 assumes possession of the account, not inheritance via a shared connection object.

anchors.ref: Obsidian Security - Integration Security for SaaS Applications
anchors.note: Notes that compromise of an iPaaS account inherits OAuth tokens for every connected app and that individually-safe integrations form toxic combinations when chained.

anchors.ref: Zapier - Manage app connections / managed apps
anchors.note: Documents the shared-connection model where flows execute under workspace connections.

GH9-ipaas-shared-connection-privilege-fa.detection: Inventory shared connections and which flows reference them; alert when a new/edited flow attaches a privileged connection or when an imported template references existing connections; bind connections to specific automations/owners and require re-authorization to attach a connection to a new flow; monitor connected-SaaS audit logs for actions from iPaaS service principals that don't match the flow's expected scope.

references.title: Obsidian Security - SaaS integration security best practices
Zapier - Manage app connections with managed apps

GH9-webextension-authenticated-session-i.mechanism: Every SaaS app a user is logged into trusts that requests carrying its session cookies/bearer tokens originate from the user's own intent in-page, while a browser extension's declared host permissions entitle it to read and act inside those same authenticated origins. An extension therefore silently inherits the user's live, MFA-satisfied sessions to every site its permissions cover. A turned-malicious extension (silent post-install update, acquired/compromised developer account, or a sleeper) uses chrome.cookies / scripting / declarativeNetRequest to harvest session cookies and bearer tokens and to drive in-page actions, impersonating the user across M365/Workspace/etc. without touching the login flow, so account takeover happens entirely after authentication and behind MFA. Bidirectional cookie injection (chrome.cookies.set) lets the attacker replay the stolen session into their own browser.
GH9-webextension-authenticated-session-i.exploitation_primitive: Ship/acquire an extension with broad host permissions that periodically reads session cookies/tokens for target SaaS origins and exfiltrates them (or injects them into an attacker browser), inheriting MFA-satisfied sessions without any credential phishing.
GH9-webextension-authenticated-session-i.mitre_gap: Closest are T1539 (Steal Web Session Cookie) and T1185 (Browser Session Hijacking), which are the nearest fit, but they do not model the browser-extension permission grant as the standing, post-auth authority that inherits every covered SaaS session, nor the supply-chain turn (silent update / acquired developer account) that flips a benign extension malicious. The ambient-authority-via-host-permissions seam is only partially captured.

anchors.ref: Socket - five malicious Chrome extensions hijacking enterprise sessions (Jan 2026)
anchors.note: 2,300+ installs; extensions requested cookies/scripting/declarativeNetRequest across Workday/NetSuite/SuccessFactors, harvested auth cookies, and offered bidirectional cookie injection for full account takeover post-MFA.

anchors.ref: Varonis - Cookie-Bite: Chrome extension steals Microsoft session tokens, bypasses MFA
anchors.note: Demonstrates an extension stealing M365 session tokens to maintain cloud access behind MFA.

GH9-webextension-authenticated-session-i.detection: Enforce extension allowlisting via browser enterprise policy and block broad host permissions / cookie access for non-vetted extensions; monitor for extension auto-updates that add permissions; detect session-token use from new device/IP fingerprints inconsistent with the user's browser (token-binding / continuous access evaluation); alert on impossible-travel reuse of a session cookie across two concurrent browsers.

references.title: CSO Online - Five Chrome extensions caught hijacking enterprise sessions
Varonis - Cookie-Bite

GH9-observability-collector-egress-remot.mechanism: An enterprise treats its telemetry pipeline (OpenTelemetry/Datadog/Fluent collector) as benign plumbing with broad host/network read reach and permission to egress to whatever exporter destinations its often remotely-managed config names. The seam is the remote-config channel (OpAMP) plus the collector's standing egress trust: an attacker who can push a config, via the OpAMP management server, a poisoned config repo, or a modified collector image, adds an attacker-controlled exporter, turning the always-trusted, egress-permitted agent into a sanctioned exfiltration channel, and simultaneously inserts processors that drop/filter security-relevant signals (e.g. removing the filelog receiver for audit logs), blinding the SOC during the operation. The pipeline's 'just observability' provenance suppresses scrutiny of its destinations and omissions.
GH9-observability-collector-egress-remot.exploitation_primitive: Push a collector config via OpAMP (or a poisoned config repo) that adds a second exporter pointing at attacker infrastructure and a filter/processor that drops alerting-relevant spans/logs, exfiltrating telemetry while blinding detection.
GH9-observability-collector-egress-remot.mitre_gap: Closest are T1567 (Exfiltration Over Web Service) and T1071.001 (Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols), but these describe generic egress, not the trusted-telemetry-pipeline-as-exfil-channel via remote config, combined with the dual signal-suppression that blinds the SOC. ATT&CK has no technique for observability-collector remote-config abuse (rogue exporter + dropped security signals) where the egress is pre-sanctioned plumbing.

anchors.ref: Systems Hardening - OTel Collector Remote Configuration Security: Hardening the OpAMP Trust Boundary
anchors.note: Describes pushing an OpAMP config to add a secondary exporter for immediate telemetry exfiltration and to silence specific alert pipelines (remove filelog receiver / drop alerting spans).

anchors.ref: OpenTelemetry OpAMP specification
anchors.note: Open Agent Management Protocol governs remote config push to collectors, the trust boundary being abused.

GH9-observability-collector-egress-remot.detection: Allowlist exporter destinations and treat any new destination as an auditable PR-gated change; run a dedicated security-critical collector with no OpAMP supervisor so it cannot receive remote config; require mTLS with a private CA on the OpAMP channel and reject unknown management servers; alert within seconds on collector config-change events and on telemetry egress to non-allowlisted endpoints, and detect drops in expected security signal volume.

references.title: Systems Hardening - OTel Collector Remote Config Security (OpAMP)
OpenTelemetry - Open Agent Management Protocol (OpAMP)

GH9-meeting-bot-autojoin-transcript-iden.mechanism: Calendar-integrated AI meeting bots (Otter, Fireflies, Read, Fathom) treat any event on a user's calendar as a legitimate invitation to attend, record, and transcribe, so they auto-join on the user's behalf via a highly permissive OAuth grant to the calendar, and downstream the org trusts the bot's transcript/summary as authoritative meeting truth feeding CRM notes, action items, and agent workflows. Two abuse paths: an injected calendar event (open invite, spoofed organizer, or compromised scheduling integration) summons a victim's note-taker into a meeting it was never meant to attend, silently exfiltrating the conversation; and content seeded into the meeting/transcript can steer the AI summary so attacker-chosen instructions or false 'agreed' decisions flow into the downstream systems that ingest the bot's output as trusted record (a prompt-injection-style data-to-control crossing).
GH9-meeting-bot-autojoin-transcript-iden.exploitation_primitive: Inject or spoof a calendar invite that triggers the victim's auto-join bot to attend and transcribe a meeting, exfiltrating the conversation; or seed transcript text engineered so the AI-generated summary/action items carry attacker-chosen 'decisions' into CRM/automation.
GH9-meeting-bot-autojoin-transcript-iden.mitre_gap: Closest are T1566 (Phishing) and T1114.002 (Email Collection: Remote Email Collection), but neither captures calendar-injection-as-summons of an auto-join AI agent, nor the downstream trust placed in AI-summarized transcripts as authoritative record. ATT&CK has no technique for AI-agent auto-join eavesdropping or transcript-summary poisoning; T1114 is email-scoped and misses the agentic meeting-capture and downstream-ingestion seam.

anchors.ref: Nudge Security - Shadow AI is taking notes: the growing risk of AI meeting assistants
anchors.note: Describes auto-join via permissive OAuth calendar grants, viral re-invite dark patterns, and bots joining every future meeting; 800 accounts in 90 days at one customer.

anchors.ref: Fireflies.ai AI note taker
anchors.note: Representative calendar-integrated auto-join meeting bot whose transcripts feed CRM/action-item workflows.

GH9-meeting-bot-autojoin-transcript-iden.detection: Govern AI-notetaker OAuth grants (least-privilege calendar scope, block auto-add-to-all-meetings); alert on bot participants joining meetings, especially externally-organized or recently-injected events; require host admit/consent for recording bots; treat AI-generated summaries as untrusted input, do not auto-execute action items into CRM/automation without human confirmation, and watch for instruction-like text in transcript-derived fields.

references.title: Nudge Security - Shadow AI is taking notes (AI meeting assistants)
University of Cambridge - Prevent AI note-taking bots from joining Teams meetings

GH9-rpa-unattended-bot-human-credential-.mechanism: Enterprise systems attribute actions performed under a user identity to that human's intent and presence, while RPA platforms run attended/unattended bots that hold and replay human or human-equivalent credentials to drive those same systems through the UI. Unattended robots must log into Windows and target apps with stored user credentials retrieved from the orchestrator's credential store (native vault or Azure Key Vault/HashiCorp integration), so the target grants the bot the impersonated identity's full authority and records every action as that person. An attacker who compromises the RPA orchestrator, a bot's credential vault, or an unattended runner harvests these standing human-equivalent credentials and drives privileged business actions through the sanctioned UI automation, and because the activity is attributed to the human, malicious actions are non-repudiably blamed on an innocent user and blend into expected automation traffic.
GH9-rpa-unattended-bot-human-credential-.exploitation_primitive: Compromise the RPA orchestrator or its credential store (or a runner host) to retrieve the stored human-equivalent credentials/tokens, then drive privileged business transactions through the bot's UI automation under the victim's identity.
GH9-rpa-unattended-bot-human-credential-.mitre_gap: Closest are T1555 (Credentials from Password Stores) and T1078.002 (Valid Accounts: Domain Accounts), but neither models the RPA-orchestrator/credential-vault as a concentration of standing human-equivalent identities, nor the non-repudiation collapse where bot actions are attributed to a human who was not present. ATT&CK has no technique for RPA bot-identity impersonation or unattended-runner credential harvesting as a business-process abuse.

anchors.ref: UiPath Orchestrator - unattended robot credentials & credential stores
anchors.note: Unattended robots require stored user credentials retrieved from the orchestrator vault (native, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp) to log into Windows and target apps, the concentrated standing-credential surface.

anchors.ref: UiPath Robot ICAM credentialing guidance
anchors.note: Vendor guidance on credentialing robots and managing their access, confirming bots hold human-equivalent credentials.

GH9-rpa-unattended-bot-human-credential-.detection: Give each robot a distinct, attributable non-human identity rather than a shared human credential; alert on orchestrator credential-asset retrievals and on robot logons outside scheduled windows/hosts; baseline per-bot action patterns and flag privileged transactions diverging from the automation's defined workflow; monitor for human-attributed sessions whose source is an RPA runner during the user's off-hours.

references.title: UiPath Orchestrator - Integrating credential stores
MITRE ATT&CK T1555 - Credentials from Password Stores

GH9-esignature-completion-event-value-re.mechanism: A business process trusts the 'agreement completed/signed' signal from an e-signature workflow (DocuSign Connect callback or a status read) as a final legal fact and wires it to release value automatically: provisioning access, paying an invoice, triggering the next agreement, or archiving the authoritative copy. The seam is the gap between 'a completion event arrived' and 'the intended parties validly agreed to this content'; an attacker subverts the completion signal rather than the per-page cryptographic seal, by forging or replaying the completion webhook (e.g. missing/weak HMAC, no timestamp/replay protection), manipulating signer-routing/recipient logic so an unauthorized party satisfies the signing step, or racing a document/template swap before the downstream system fetches the completed artifact, so the receiving system releases value on a procedurally invalid completion even though each tamper-sealed page checks out.
GH9-esignature-completion-event-value-re.exploitation_primitive: Forge/replay the DocuSign Connect completion webhook (or manipulate recipient routing / swap the template before fetch) so the downstream system treats the envelope as validly completed and releases value (access, payment, next-step trigger).
GH9-esignature-completion-event-value-re.mitre_gap: Closest are T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1565.001 (Stored Data Manipulation), but neither captures completion-event-as-value-release trust, the business-logic seam where a procedurally-invalid 'completed' signal triggers downstream value despite valid page seals. ATT&CK has no technique for e-signature completion-callback forgery/recipient-routing abuse; T1190 implies a code exploit, this is trusted-event business-logic abuse.

anchors.ref: DocuSign Connect webhooks / HMAC verification
anchors.note: Connect completion events drive downstream provisioning/order/payment automation; vendor guidance requires HMAC verification plus replay protection (timestamp/unique ID), absence of which enables forgery/replay.

anchors.ref: eSignGlobal - DocuSign Connect: securing webhooks with HMAC signature verification
anchors.note: Details HMAC verification and replay-protection practices for completion callbacks; the abuse condition is their omission.

GH9-esignature-completion-event-value-re.detection: Verify the Connect HMAC over the raw body and enforce timestamp/idempotency replay protection; never act on the callback alone, re-fetch envelope status and the completed artifact (by envelope ID and document hash) from the e-signature API before releasing value; validate recipient-routing/signer identity against the intended parties; alert on completion events whose envelope/document hash differs from the originally-sent document.

references.title: DocuSign Connect (webhooks)
eSignGlobal - Securing DocuSign Connect webhooks with HMAC

GH9-citizen-dev-flow-privileged-connecto.mechanism: Low-code/no-code platforms (Microsoft Power Platform) let a flow built by any citizen developer run under whatever connection reference is attached to it, so when an admin shares a privileged service connection (or a flow) into a maker environment, every automation referencing it executes with that connection's elevated authority on the assumption the maker was implicitly entitled to it. This indirect (implicit) use of a connection circumvents the maker's own access rights, and the platform logs the action under the service connection identity rather than the human who directed it. A citizen developer, an attacker who took over a maker account, or a planted shared flow/template can author an automation that references the admin's privileged connection to read restricted data, write to production, or create identities, silently elevating a low-privilege author to the connection owner's authority. DLP policy historically enforced only on new connections, leaving large back-catalogs of existing shared connections exploitable.
GH9-citizen-dev-flow-privileged-connecto.exploitation_primitive: As a low-privilege maker (or via a compromised maker account / planted shared template), build a flow that references an admin's shared privileged connection and use its stored authority to perform actions beyond your own rights; the action is logged as the connection, not you.
GH9-citizen-dev-flow-privileged-connecto.mitre_gap: Closest are T1078.004 (Valid Accounts: Cloud) and T1098 (Account Manipulation), but neither models low-code shared-connector authority borrowing, where the platform executes a flow with a connection owner's privilege regardless of the author's rights, and attribution is to the connection not the human. ATT&CK has no technique for citizen-dev privileged-connector borrow / implicit-connection privilege escalation.

anchors.ref: Secureworks (CTU) - Power Platform Privilege Escalation
anchors.note: Demonstrates privilege escalation via the Power Platform API by elevating an existing service principal; shows the platform as a privilege-escalation target.

anchors.ref: Zenity Labs - The Long and Winding Road of DLP Patches in Power Platform
anchors.note: Documents that DLP restrictions were enforced only on new connections, not existing ones, leaving shared connections borrowable.

GH9-citizen-dev-flow-privileged-connecto.detection: Inventory shared connections/connection references and which flows and makers consume them; alert on a flow referencing a connection whose owner differs from the author, and on newly imported shared flows/templates; enforce DLP on existing (not just new) connections; correlate connected-system audit logs for service-connection actions that exceed the directing maker's own entitlements.

references.title: Secureworks - Power Platform Privilege Escalation
Zenity Labs - DLP Patches in Power Platform

GH6-oracle-twap-window-cross-block-warp.mechanism: A TWAP oracle (e.g. Uniswap V3's geometric-mean accumulator) integrates price over an observation window by summing tick*time between checkpoints; the 'time' weight of each price is the number of seconds (effectively blocks) that price persisted. When a single actor controls consecutive block production - a PoS proposer who gets two adjacent slots, an L2 sequencer with unilateral ordering, or anyone exploiting long/irregular block intervals - they can push a low-liquidity pool's spot price in block N and revert it in block N+1, so a manipulated tick dominates the time-weighting with no arbitrage exposure between the two blocks. The averaging that the consumer treats as manipulation-resistant ground truth is defeated by warping the time dimension (which blocks land in-window, and for how long) rather than by overpowering the prices. Post-Merge this is materially cheaper because a proposer can back-run their own manipulation in a guaranteed multi-block sequence.
GH6-oracle-twap-window-cross-block-warp.exploitation_primitive: Acquire two consecutive blocks (proposer/builder via MEV markets or sole L2 sequencer), spike a thin pool's price in the first block, read the now-skewed TWAP from a lending/liquidation contract in the same or next block, then unwind - extracting collateral or triggering liquidations against the warped average.
GH6-oracle-twap-window-cross-block-warp.mitre_gap: ATT&CK Enterprise has no technique for on-chain price-oracle or TWAP manipulation; there is no smart-contract data-source tactic. The nearest analogue, T1565 Data Manipulation (Stored/Transmitted), describes tampering with data at rest or in transit on conventional hosts and assumes an integrity boundary an attacker breaches - it cannot express manipulation of a value that is honestly computed from a public AMM whose pricing the attacker legitimately moves with capital and block-ordering control, so it is insufficient.

anchors.ref: Euler Finance - Uniswap V3 TWAP manipulation cost-of-attack
anchors.note: Formal cost-of-attack analysis and simulator quantifying single- vs multi-block TWAP manipulation; shows feasibility hinges on low in-range liquidity and proposer multi-block control.

anchors.ref: Uniswap Labs - Uniswap v3 TWAP Oracles in Proof of Stake
anchors.note: Uniswap's own analysis of how PoS multi-block proposer control reduces TWAP manipulation cost and recommended window/liquidity mitigations.

anchors.ref: Mango Markets oracle manipulation ($117M, Oct 2022)
anchors.note: Real-world oracle price manipulation: thin MNGO liquidity let the attacker pump the oracle-referenced price ~2300% and borrow against inflated collateral; demonstrates the economic class even though the warp vector here was liquidity not block cadence.

GH6-oracle-twap-window-cross-block-warp.detection: Off-chain: monitor for the same EOA/proposer appearing as fee_recipient/coinbase across two consecutive blocks containing a large swap and its inverse on a low-liquidity pool, plus an oracle-consuming call (liquidation/borrow) sandwiched between them. On-chain: alert when a pool's spot price deviates sharply from its own longer TWAP (e.g. >X% spot-vs-30min) within the same block an oracle read occurs, or when in-range liquidity collapses toward zero right before a price spike.

references.title: Euler - uni-v3-twap-manipulation (cost-of-attack)
Uniswap v3 TWAP Oracles in Proof of Stake
Mango Markets exploit explainer (CoinDesk)

GH6-bridge-lightclient-finality-reorg-mi.mechanism: A lock-and-mint bridge runs an on-destination light client (or relayer set) that accepts a source-chain deposit once the included header/proof reaches a hard-coded confirmation depth, then irreversibly mints the wrapped asset. The depth is a static assumption about finality, but real finality is probabilistic (PoW depth) or reconfigurable (PoS finality gadgets can stall, validator sets rotate, and some L2s have soft 'finality' that lags L1 settlement). If the source chain reorgs deeper than the configured depth - or a finality-gadget stall is resolved on a fork that orphans the proven block - the source-side Lock is rolled back while the destination-side Mint persists, producing unbacked wrapped tokens. The seam is the bridge equating its local confirmation count with a single global notion of irreversibility the source chain never actually guaranteed at that depth.
GH6-bridge-lightclient-finality-reorg-mi.exploitation_primitive: Deposit on a source chain near a known reorg-prone condition (short-block PoW chain, contentious fork, or a chain where the attacker can induce/await a deep reorg), let the destination mint at the bridge's fixed depth, then cause or wait out a reorg that reverts the source deposit while keeping the minted wrapped asset - a cross-chain double-spend.
GH6-bridge-lightclient-finality-reorg-mi.mitre_gap: ATT&CK has no concept of blockchain finality, chain reorganization, or cross-chain double-spend; no tactic models trust in a consensus-finality assumption. The loosest analogue is T1565.001 Stored Data Manipulation, but a reorg is not the attacker corrupting stored data - it is the source ledger legitimately rewriting its own history per its consensus rules while a second system already acted on the rewritten state, which ATT&CK's host-centric integrity model cannot represent.

anchors.ref: Across Protocol - A Guide to Finality Risk
anchors.note: Practitioner analysis of finality risk for bridges: confirmation-depth choices vs probabilistic/reconfigurable finality and the reorg double-mint scenario.

anchors.ref: Double-Spending Attacks in Cross-Blockchain Ecosystems (ScienceDirect)
anchors.note: Peer-reviewed treatment of reorg-driven double-spend across linked chains via bridges, formalizing the confirmation-depth vs actual-finality gap.

anchors.ref: LI.FI - Ethereum, Finality and Bridges
anchors.note: Explains why honoring source events before finality (or with optimistic depth) lets a later reorg remove the Lock while the Mint remains, yielding unbacked assets.

GH6-bridge-lightclient-finality-reorg-mi.detection: On-chain: cross-monitor source and destination - flag any destination mint whose corresponding source deposit transaction/log no longer exists at the canonical source height (proof references an orphaned block hash). Off-chain: track source-chain reorg depth in real time and raise alerts when observed reorg depth approaches or exceeds the bridge's configured confirmation depth; reconcile total wrapped supply against escrowed source collateral and alert on any positive divergence.

references.title: Across - Dodging the Finality Bullet: A Guide to Finality Risk
Double-Spending Attacks in Cross-Blockchain Ecosystems
LI.FI - Ethereum, Finality and Bridges

GH6-proxy-upgrade-storage-collision-sile.mechanism: An upgradeable proxy uses delegatecall so the implementation's code executes against the proxy's persistent storage. Storage variables are assigned sequential slots by the Solidity compiler at compile time; the proxy and successive implementations must agree on this layout or the same slot is reinterpreted. When a new implementation inserts, reorders, or changes the type of a state variable (or the proxy itself declares its own variable colliding with the logic's slot 0), the raw bytes already in a slot - once a balance or a boolean 'initialized' flag - are silently read as something else, e.g. an owner address or a role bit. EIP-1967 fixes only the proxy's admin/implementation pointers to pseudo-random slots; it does not protect against collisions between implementation versions, which remain the developer's responsibility. The trusted address is bytecode-immutable in appearance while its meaning is reassigned underneath.
GH6-proxy-upgrade-storage-collision-sile.exploitation_primitive: Ship (or, with a captured admin key, push) an implementation whose layout reuses an occupied slot so an attacker-controlled value lands in the owner/role/initialized slot - e.g. a proxy-declared admin variable colliding with the logic's initialized flag - then re-invoke initialize() or a privileged function to seize governance/treasury.
GH6-proxy-upgrade-storage-collision-sile.mitre_gap: ATT&CK has no technique for storage-slot reinterpretation in a delegatecall proxy; the closest analogue, T1574 Hijack Execution Flow (and its DLL/path-precedence subtechniques), is about substituting which code runs, whereas here the code is reviewed and immutable and the attack is the reinterpretation of persistent data under a new-but-honest implementation - an EVM storage-layout failure ATT&CK has no vocabulary for.

anchors.ref: Audius governance takeover (~$6M, Jul 2022)
anchors.note: Demonstrated: a proxy-declared proxyAdmin variable collided with the logic's initialized flag, so the non-zero admin address read as 'not initialized'; attacker re-called initialize(), became governor, and drained the treasury. Confirms the 'demonstrated' rating.

anchors.ref: EIP-1967: Proxy Storage Slots
anchors.note: Standardizes proxy admin/implementation to pseudo-random slots to avoid proxy<->logic clashes, but explicitly does not solve logic-version-to-version layout collisions.

anchors.ref: OpenZeppelin Upgrades - Proxy Upgrade Pattern (storage gaps / layout rules)
anchors.note: Documents that storage must be append-only across versions and that the plugin detects layout collisions; the canonical defensive guidance.

GH6-proxy-upgrade-storage-collision-sile.detection: Pre-deploy: run OpenZeppelin Upgrades storage-layout diff / validateUpgrade against the prior implementation and block any non-append-only change; CI gate on layout hash. On-chain: monitor Upgraded(address) events on critical proxies and, on each upgrade, diff the new vs old storage layout and re-read owner/admin/role slots, alerting if a previously value-bearing slot now resolves to a privileged role or if initialize() becomes callable again.

references.title: Audius Governance Takeover Post-Mortem 7/23/22
EIP-1967: Proxy Storage Slots
OpenZeppelin - Proxy Upgrade Pattern

GH6-eip712-domain-replay-fork-chainid-co.mechanism: EIP-712 binds a typed-data signature to a context via a domainSeparator hashed from name, version, chainId, and verifyingContract. The signature only authorizes the intended context if all four fields actually pin it. Failures arise when chainId is omitted or cached at deploy time (so it is wrong after a fork/reorg or shared across an EVM-compatible chain), when verifyingContract is deployed at the identical address on a second chain via CREATE2/deterministic factories, or when a contract or wallet caches a stale separator. In those cases the on-chain ecrecover validates a signature the human scoped to one chain/contract against a colliding context, executing value movement the signer never authorized there. The seam is the multichain address/chainId namespace the user never reasoned about while the contract treats the signature as scoped proof of intent.
GH6-eip712-domain-replay-fork-chainid-co.exploitation_primitive: Capture a valid EIP-712 message (permit, gasless order, intent) and replay it where the domain failed to pin - a forked/rollup chain reusing the historical chainId, or a verifying contract redeployed at the same address on a second chain - so one signature drains value in a context the signer never reasoned about.
GH6-eip712-domain-replay-fork-chainid-co.mitre_gap: ATT&CK has no technique for cryptographic-signature domain confusion or cross-chain replay; nothing models a signed authorization whose scope-binding silently collides across ledgers. The nearest analogues, T1550.001/.004 Use Alternate Authentication Material (application access tokens / web session cookies), assume a stolen credential reused within one system's auth boundary - they cannot express a correctly-scoped EIP-712 signature being honored in a parallel chain context the signer's domain separator failed to exclude.

anchors.ref: Optimism / Wintermute replay (~20M OP, Jun 2022)
anchors.note: Demonstrated cross-chain replay: a Gnosis Safe factory/deployment from L1 Ethereum was replayed on Optimism (L2 reusing the same address/context), letting an attacker deploy the multisig and seize 20M OP allocated to an address not yet deployed on L2.

anchors.ref: EIP-712: Typed structured data hashing and signing
anchors.note: Defines the domainSeparator (name/version/chainId/verifyingContract) whose purpose is exactly to prevent cross-chain and cross-contract signature replay - and whose misuse causes this class.

anchors.ref: ERC-5267: Retrieval of EIP-712 domain
anchors.note: Standard for exposing the active domain so wallets/integrators can detect drift and avoid signing against a stale or cross-chain-colliding separator.

GH6-eip712-domain-replay-fork-chainid-co.detection: Contract: detect domainSeparator caching by checking whether chainId is read at signature-verification time vs frozen at deploy; flag verifiers that never recompute on chainId change. On-chain: monitor for the identical signature (r,s,v) or identical struct hash being submitted on more than one chain or against a same-address contract on another chain. Off-chain: wallet-side, compare the signed domain (via ERC-5267 eip712Domain()) against the live chainId/verifyingContract and warn on mismatch before signing.

references.title: SlowMist - Theft of 20M OP Tokens via Transaction Replay
EIP-712: Typed structured data hashing and signing
ERC-5267: Retrieval of EIP-712 domain

GH6-blind-signing-simulation-divergence-.mechanism: Wallet clear-signing previews run a transaction simulation (eth_call/trace against current state, or a forked state) and render predicted asset deltas and decoded calldata. The prediction is only valid if simulated state equals execution state, but those diverge: the simulation reads block.timestamp/number, oracle prices, balances, or storage flags at simulation time, while the mined transaction reads them at inclusion time. An attacker crafts calldata that branches on something the simulator does not reproduce - a value that changes between check and use (TOCTOU), a front-run state mutation, a flash-set storage flag, or tx.origin/gasleft-dependent logic - so the benign, profitable-looking preview is honest for the simulated state yet the executed path drains the signer. The defense built to end blind signing becomes the deception surface.
GH6-blind-signing-simulation-divergence-.exploitation_primitive: Deploy a contract whose call simulates as 'you receive X, send Y' but whose executed branch (gated on timestamp, an oracle read, a pre-inclusion state change, or a hidden flag) instead approves/transfers the victim's assets, then induce the user to approve based on the decoy preview.
GH6-blind-signing-simulation-divergence-.mitre_gap: ATT&CK has no technique for transaction-simulation evasion or preview/execution divergence; no tactic models a security-preview oracle being gamed by state that differs at execution time. The nearest analogues are T1497 Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion and T1036 Masquerading - but those describe malware detecting an analysis sandbox or disguising a file, whereas here a legitimate on-chain simulation is honest yet non-representative of inclusion-time state, a TOCTOU property of EVM execution ATT&CK does not capture.

anchors.ref: Blockaid - Dissecting TOCTOU Attacks: Wallet Drainers Exploit Transaction Timing
anchors.note: Documents drainers altering or gating on state between simulation and execution so a hidden condition not triggered during simulation drains assets on inclusion.

anchors.ref: Blockaid - Malicious dApps 101: Wallet Drainers
anchors.note: Describes simulation-based deception (EIP-712 offline signatures, Permit2/session-key obfuscation) used to bypass preview-based detection.

anchors.ref: MetaMask Security Alerts (Blockaid) documentation
anchors.note: Vendor documentation of the simulation/clear-signing preview model wallets rely on, establishing the trusted layer this technique subverts.

GH6-blind-signing-simulation-divergence-.detection: Off-chain: re-simulate immediately before broadcast and at multiple block heights/states; flag transactions whose predicted asset deltas are unstable across re-simulations or that branch on block.timestamp/number, gasleft, tx.origin, or freshly-written storage flags. On-chain/mempool: compare actual post-execution balance changes against the wallet's pre-signed simulated deltas and alert on divergence; treat interactions with newly-deployed, unverified contracts that pass simulation but contain timestamp/oracle-gated branches as high risk.

references.title: Blockaid - Dissecting TOCTOU Attacks
Blockaid - Malicious dApps 101: Wallet Drainers
MetaMask - Security alerts (Blockaid)

GH6-did-credential-status-staleness-trus.mechanism: A verifier of a W3C Verifiable Credential checks the issuer signature plus a referenced status mechanism - a Bitstring/StatusList credential (a compressed bitstring where each credential maps to one bit: 1=revoked), an on-chain revocation registry, or did:method resolution. Validity is read from a snapshot the verifier fetches and typically caches, but a decentralized identity system has no synchronous authority that answers 'is this valid right now': status lists are published/mirrored with latency, registries propagate across nodes, and DID document resolution can return cached or stale results. An attacker presents a credential after revocation but before the cached status list/registry/DID document the verifier sees reflects it - or steers the verifier to a stale mirror - so a revoked or rotated credential is honored as currently active. The verifier trusts a lagging snapshot as present-tense truth.
GH6-did-credential-status-staleness-trus.exploitation_primitive: Present a recently-revoked or key-rotated credential during the propagation window, or against a verifier pinned to a cached/outdated status-list mirror or stale DID resolution, so the status bit still reads 'active' and access is granted.
GH6-did-credential-status-staleness-trus.mitre_gap: ATT&CK has no technique for decentralized-credential revocation lag or DID status staleness; no tactic models trust in an eventually-consistent status layer with no synchronous authority. The nearest analogues, T1606 Forge Web Credentials and T1550 Use Alternate Authentication Material, assume a forged or stolen token, whereas here a genuinely-issued-then-revoked credential is honored because the distributed status source the verifier consulted had not yet converged - an availability/consistency gap in decentralized PKI ATT&CK does not represent.

anchors.ref: W3C Bitstring Status List v1.0 (Recommendation)
anchors.note: Defines the bitstring revocation/suspension mechanism whose publish-and-cache model creates the propagation-latency window this technique exploits.

anchors.ref: W3C Verifiable Credentials Status List 2021 (predecessor draft)
anchors.note: Earlier StatusList2021 draft; notes it was experimental/under heavy development, underscoring immature, non-synchronous status resolution in deployed systems.

GH6-did-credential-status-staleness-trus.detection: Verifier-side: enforce a maximum status-list cache TTL and a freshness/validUntil check on fetched status credentials; reject or step-up when the status list's issuance/refresh timestamp is older than a policy threshold. Fetch status from the issuer's canonical endpoint (not an arbitrary mirror) and pin the DID document version; log and alert when a credential validated against a status snapshot that is later found to have been stale (post-hoc reconciliation against the issuer's authoritative current list).

references.title: W3C Bitstring Status List v1.0
W3C Verifiable Credentials Status List v2021 (WD)

GH6-dao-governance-flashstake-snapshot-c.mechanism: On-chain governance measures voting weight from a token/vote-escrow/delegated balance at a proposal's snapshot block and equates that point-in-time weight with durable, at-risk economic stake. Because ERC-20 governance tokens are freely transferable and DeFi offers atomic flash loans, an attacker can borrow an enormous balance, satisfy the snapshot within a single transaction, and unwind in the same block - holding no lasting stake. If voting and execution lack a time delay (or the snapshot is taken at the current block rather than a historically-checkpointed past block), the attacker reads attacker-controlled weight as legitimate and passes a self-dealing proposal. The seam is governance trusting a measured balance as a proxy for persistent alignment it never verifies the persistence of.
GH6-dao-governance-flashstake-snapshot-c.exploitation_primitive: In one transaction, flash-borrow or momentarily acquire >quorum governance/vote-escrow weight, vote on and (via emergency/no-delay execution) execute a malicious proposal transferring treasury funds, then repay the flash loan - all atomically.
GH6-dao-governance-flashstake-snapshot-c.mitre_gap: ATT&CK has no technique for governance-token flash-stake or snapshot capture; nothing models voting-weight measurement as a trust boundary or atomic borrowed-capital manipulation of a decision process. There is no meaningful analogue - T1199 Trusted Relationship and account-manipulation techniques assume persistent illegitimate access, whereas this is a single-block, fully-repaid acquisition of legitimate-looking voting power with zero residual stake, which ATT&CK's identity/access model cannot express.

anchors.ref: Beanstalk Farms governance flash-loan attack ($182M, Apr 2022)
anchors.note: Canonical demonstrated case: attacker flash-borrowed ~$1B to obtain >2/3 Stalk voting power and called emergencyCommit to execute a malicious BIP in the same transaction, with no flash-loan-resistant vote measurement and no vote/execute delay.

anchors.ref: OpenZeppelin Governor / ERC-20Votes checkpointing
anchors.note: Defensive design: past-block snapshot voting power (getPastVotes) plus voting delay/timelock so weight must be held at a prior block and execution is delayed, defeating same-block flash-stake.

GH6-dao-governance-flashstake-snapshot-c.detection: On-chain: flag any vote cast where the voter's governance-token balance was acquired (transfer-in or flash-loan callback) within the same transaction or block as the snapshot, especially if balance drops to ~0 immediately after; alert on proposals reaching quorum from addresses with near-zero historical holding-time. Monitor for emergencyCommit/execute occurring in the same transaction as vote casting, and for flash-loan provider calls (Aave/Uniswap/Balancer) bracketing a governance interaction.

references.title: Immunefi - Hack Analysis: Beanstalk Governance Attack, April 2022
OpenZeppelin - Governance (Governor, ERC20Votes)

GH6-erc4337-paymaster-validation-executi.mechanism: ERC-4337 separates a UserOperation into a validation phase (account and paymaster check signatures/policy/allowances via validatePaymasterUserOp) and a later execution phase (the account's call and the paymaster's postOp). A paymaster commits to sponsoring gas based on state observed during validation - the userOp calldata, account state, an off-chain signed sponsorship policy, or an ERC-20 allowance it will pull as repayment. Because execution happens after validation and bundlers may order/aggregate many userOps, the relied-upon state can change before postOp runs: the repayment token's balance/allowance is drained, the sponsored action is altered via ordering/reentrancy effects, or aggregated ops interfere. The paymaster then pays gas for an operation that no longer matches what it validated, and at scale its EntryPoint deposit is drained. ERC-7562 storage/opcode rules and staking constrain mempool-level griefing but do not eliminate validation-time-vs-execution-time state divergence the paymaster economically depends on.
GH6-erc4337-paymaster-validation-executi.exploitation_primitive: Submit userOps that validate against a paymaster's token-repayment or policy check, then within the same bundle/execution change that state (drain the repayment-token balance/allowance, or reorder a reentrant action) so postOp cannot collect, forcing the paymaster to eat the gas - repeated to drain its deposit.
GH6-erc4337-paymaster-validation-executi.mitre_gap: ATT&CK has no technique for account-abstraction paymaster economics or validation/execution TOCTOU; no tactic models a gas-sponsorship contract paying for an operation whose preconditions lapsed between phases. The closest analogue is T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service (resource exhaustion), but that targets service availability via traffic, whereas this is a contract-protocol economic drain exploiting ERC-4337's phase separation and bundler ordering - an on-chain mechanism ATT&CK does not model.

anchors.ref: ERC-4337: Account Abstraction Using Alt Mempool
anchors.note: Defines the validation/execution phase separation, paymaster sponsorship, EntryPoint deposit, and postOp repayment - the structure this technique abuses.

anchors.ref: ERC-7562: Validation rules for AA (mempool/storage-access rules)
anchors.note: Specifies storage/opcode restrictions and staking/reputation that bundlers enforce to limit paymaster griefing and mass invalidation; documents the boundary and its limits.

anchors.ref: OpenZeppelin - ERC-4337 Account Abstraction Incremental Audit
anchors.note: Audit of reference AA contracts including paymaster validation/postOp gas accounting and griefing protections, evidencing the divergence risk class.

GH6-erc4337-paymaster-validation-executi.detection: On-chain: monitor a paymaster's EntryPoint deposit balance and alert on abnormal drain rate or on postOp reverts/failed repayments clustered from few accounts/factories. Correlate userOps where the repayment token's balanceOf/allowance for the sender dropped between the validation block-state and execution; flag bundles whose userOps share a sponsoring paymaster and mutate each other's relied-upon state. Off-chain: track per-sender sponsorship cost vs successful repayment ratio and rate-limit or de-sponsor senders whose execution-phase state repeatedly diverges from validation.

references.title: ERC-4337: Account Abstraction Using Alt Mempool
ERC-7562 - ERC-4337 Documentation
OpenZeppelin - ERC-4337 Account Abstraction Incremental Audit

GH6-depin-proof-of-physical-work-sensor-.mechanism: A DePIN reward contract mints tokens in proportion to submitted proofs of physical work - sensor readings, GNSS/location attestations, coverage/bandwidth measurements, hashed device telemetry, or witness attestations. On-chain, the contract can verify a proof is cryptographically well-formed but cannot verify it corresponds to real-world work at a real place and time, because the gap between the digital attestation and physical ground truth is unobservable from the chain. Attackers spoof GNSS coordinates, replay or synthesize sensor telemetry, virtualize many device identities on one host, or collude so witnesses attest to non-existent coverage. The contract mints because a well-formed fabrication is indistinguishable from a well-formed honest proof; mitigations (e.g. Helium's trust-score/denylist, multi-witness proof-of-coverage) are heuristic and off-chain, not a cryptographic guarantee of physicality.
GH6-depin-proof-of-physical-work-sensor-.exploitation_primitive: Spoof a device's asserted location (GNSS spoofing) and/or replay synthetic coverage/sensor proofs - optionally clustering many virtual device identities so they 'witness' each other - to mint rewards for coverage or work that never physically occurred.
GH6-depin-proof-of-physical-work-sensor-.mitre_gap: ATT&CK Enterprise has no technique for proof-of-physical-work, GNSS/location spoofing, or DePIN reward fraud; no tactic models a blockchain minting value from unverifiable physical-world attestations. The nearest analogues live outside Enterprise - ATT&CK for ICS lacks an oracle/minting concept, and T1565 Data Manipulation assumes corrupting data inside a system - whereas here the proof is authentic-looking and the falsity is in its correspondence to a physical reality the chain cannot observe, which no ATT&CK technique captures.

anchors.ref: Helium Proof-of-Coverage location spoofing / network gaming
anchors.note: Documented real-world gaming: spoofers assert false GPS locations and cluster self-witnessing hotspots to mint HNT for non-existent coverage; persistent despite denylist enforcement.

anchors.ref: Helium HIP-66: Trust Score and Denylist
anchors.note: Helium Improvement Proposal introducing a multi-signal trust score to flag likely-spoofing hotspots - evidence the problem is mitigated heuristically off-chain, not solved on-chain.

GH6-depin-proof-of-physical-work-sensor-.detection: Off-chain analytics: cross-check asserted location against RF/timing plausibility (witness RSSI/SNR vs distance, signal-propagation feasibility), detect impossible co-location or hotspots that only ever witness a fixed cluster, and correlate many device identities to one IP/ASN/host. Flag GNSS attestations inconsistent with multi-constellation/anti-spoof signals or with abrupt teleportation between assertions. On-chain: alert on reward concentration to owner clusters and on devices whose proofs lack independent third-party witnesses.

references.title: Helium Hotspot Spoofing and the Deny List
Helium HIP-66: Trust Score and Denylist
